issue_title: Deal better with approximate/uncertain dates
issue_body:
Currently, a fixed (precision-dependent) and arbitrary amount of fuzziness is added to approximate/uncertain dates. This is used to return a date range that can be used for filtering, but it's a pretty basic interpretation.

Properly, the level of uncertainty will be a curve that ramps continuously between 1.0 ("the given date is a certain match for the EDTF range") to 0.0 ("the given date is certainly not a match").

Open questions:
* How are these fuzzy ramps defined? We might get some non-arbitrary results from crowdsourcing interpretations of whether a given date is 'circa' another date, etc. But we're likely to want to find a simple ramping equation.
* Are curves significantly more useful than just padding a strict date range? It will allow us to return queries to "Show me works made around 1929" sorted by confidence. Is that a real-world use case?

issue_title: Declaring traittypes in Containers as uninstantiated classes
issue_body:
Feel free to ignore this, but I wasn't around when #51 was discussed… and this just came up in jupyter/nbconvert#695. So I wanted to weigh in on the question around deprecating a particular way of using uninstantiated traitlets. There are two ways of using traitlets that were deprecated in 4.1 that were not documented in the migration docs http://traitlets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/migration.html. These use cases involve traitlets being used in slightly different ways. I think we should revisit the decision to deprecate one of them. Regardless of whether we continue to deprecate that case, the deprecations should be mentioned in the migration docs.
Click for more details about these two cases and why I think they are different.

 1. traitlets that are actually instantiated on a class. ```python class MyClass(): foo = Unicode() # ✓ foo = Unicode # deprecated ``` 2. traitlets defining which types are valid inside traitlets subclassed from `Container` (e.g., `List`). ```python class MyClass(): foo = List(Unicode()) # ✓ foo = List(Unicode) # deprecated ``` Deprecating 1. was absolutely the right call. I think that we should revisit the idea of deprecating 2..
Click here for my argument as to why we shouldn't deprecate use case 2.

In case 2, the actual use of a traitlet instance is somewhat strange from a type checking perspective. What you are effectively doing is declaring that the elements of this container need to be of the same _type_ as element that is listed. The only way I've ever seen type checking work involves declaring the _type_s that should be allowed, in python that would be the _class_es that should be allowed. To follow the requirement that we only allow traitlet instances there would be analogous to a type checking syntax that declared that something should be a string by using literal strings. For example, the analogous case from the python type [annotations PEP][pytypes] was: ```python def greeting(name: str) -> str: return 'Hello ' + name ``` but if using our convention it would be ```python def greeting(name: str()) -> str(): return 'Hello ' + name ``` or equivalently: ```python def greeting(name: '') -> '': return 'Hello ' + name ``` I realise that from an implementation point of view this introduces overhead because we also need to be able to put constraints on particular values, which means we can't only work on classes.
**tldr**:; The use case in 2. is similar to type checking, and declaring types as uninstantiated classes is a standard way of specifying types in signatures (including in [python type annotations][pytypes]).
[pytypes]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/

issue_title: TimeDeltaParameter can't be passed to dynamic dependencies
issue_body:
I have a task with a TimeDeltaParameter, let's call it TaskA. I can run TaskA from the command line like so
```
luigi --module project.modules TaskA --buffer '1 minute'
```
TaskA has a dynamic dependency, TaskB, which I yield from the run function like so
```
def run():
    yield TaskB(self.buffer)
```
This results in the error
```
luigi.parameter.ParameterException: Invalid time delta - could not parse 0:01:00
```
It seems like passing TimeDeltaParameters to dynamic dependencies results in this issue, because they are passed as strings and then can't be parsed. Even more interesting, is when requiring TaskB from the requires function, it works just fine. Digging deeper, it looks like the parse function which throws the above error, isn't called when running dependencies from the requires function. Does anyone know how to get around this? I'm also curious as to if there's any way to set a default TimeDeltaParameter of 0 days as that seems to throw the same error as above.

issue_title: Visualizer fails with similar range targets.
issue_body:
I'm using Range Targets to guide the loading of some information that appears on a daily basis that we want to load ASAP. (We have the configuration set to retry on external task failures and to keep the workers alive)

When you run two Range Targets with the same parameters at the same time, but the target task has different parameters, the visualizer will only display one of the range targets in any given screen. The sub-tasks are of course still present and Luigi doesn't appear to have any issue with the Range targets being identical, so I am presuming this is an okay way to use Range. I don't know enough about JS to dig in and figure out what's going wrong, but I imagine it has something to do with the task parameters being identical.

My command lines look something like this:

```
# Note that I use 2016.04.01 to mean, start loading today's data. This is set in our cron job to change to whatever the current date is.
# 2 Days forward takes care of any issues with the international date line
luigi --module mymodule RangeDaily --start 2016.04.01 --days-forward 2 --of MyTask --MyTask-myparam 1
luigi --module mymodule RangeDaily --start 2016.04.01 --days-forward 2 --of MyTask --MyTask-myparam 2
```

Am I just going about this backassward? I feel like this would be an issue if you had to run a range on a parameterized task for any reason. Just to reiterate, there's *no* problem with the jobs running or anything, this only shows up as an issue in the visualizer.

issue_title: Datastore does not work well with WebUI
issue_body:
New calculators which are using the 'datastore' feature does not work well with the WebUI in a server/multiuser installation.

Since in a multiuser installation the WebUI process is not started by the end user that is actually running the computation, the datastore is written in the webserver process owner's homedir instead of the calculation user's home. In any case the datastore cannot be created on the users' home because lack of privileges.

When an end user tries to run a risk computation on top of a previously calculated hazard it will fails because it tries to find the datastore in the user's home, instead it's in the WebUI process owner's home.

Calculation run through the WebUI should check for the datastore in the process owner's home, even if this will break cross-compatibility with the command line: previously was possible to run the hazard from the command line and then the risk from the WebUI and vv.

This has been marked as critical since the WebUI on Wilson (run by the 'openquake' user) will be used next weel in the Risk workshop to run hazard + risk.

issue_title: Change TranscribeService.Client.start_transcription_job() limit (10 jobs only)
issue_body:
Hi there,

many thanks for your `boto3` Pyton lib. I am working on auditio transcription ([odigo-auditor](https://github.com/korniichuk/odigo-auditor) open source lib) based on [Amazon Trinscribe](https://aws.amazon.com/transcribe/). How can I start more than 10  asynchronous job with  [TranscribeService.Client.start_transcription_job()](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/transcribe.html#TranscribeService.Client.start_transcription_job) boto3 function?
It is mean, that I run [TranscribeService.Client.start_transcription_job()](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/transcribe.html#TranscribeService.Client.start_transcription_job) function 123 times but [Amazon Trinscribe](https://aws.amazon.com/transcribe/) created only 10 first jobs.
Source code:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python2
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import urlparse
from os.path import basename, join

import boto3

bucket_name = 'transcribe.odigo-auditor'

def transcribe_dir(src, dst=None, language_code='en-US'):
    """Transcribe all mp3 file/files from directory (recursively) on AWS S3 with
       AWS Transcribe.
    Input:
        src -- S3 location of the dir (required | type: str). Example:
               'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/';
        dst -- S3 bucket where the transcription is stored (not required |
               type: str);
        language_code -- language code for the language used in the input mp3
                         file (not required | type: str | default: 'en-US').

    """

    while src.startswith('/'):
        src = src[1:]
    if not src.startswith('http'):
        bucket_name = src.split('/')[0]
        dir_name =  src.replace(bucket_name, '')
        while dir_name.startswith('/'):
            dir_name = dir_name[1:]
        location = boto3.client('s3') \
                .get_bucket_location(Bucket=bucket_name)['LocationConstraint']
        base_url = join('https://s3-%s.amazonaws.com' % location, bucket_name)
    else:
        path = urlparse.urlparse(src).path
        while path.startswith('/'):
            path = path[1:]
        bucket_name = path.split('/')[0]
        dir_name = path.replace(bucket_name, '')
        while dir_name.startswith('/'):
            dir_name = dir_name[1:]
        base_url = join('https://%s' % urlparse.urlparse(src).netloc,
                        bucket_name)
    for bucket in boto3.resource('s3').buckets.all():
        if bucket.name == bucket_name:
            for key in bucket.objects.all():
                if key.key.startswith(dir_name) and key.key.endswith('.mp3'):
                    url = join(base_url, key.key)
                    transcribe_mp3(url, dst)

def transcribe_mp3(src, dst=None, job_name=None, language_code='en-US'):
    """Transcribe mp3 file with AWS Transcribe.
    Input:
        src -- S3 location of the src mp3 file (required | type: str). Example:
               'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/example.mp3';
        dst -- S3 bucket where the transcription is stored (not required |
               type: str);
        job_name -- the name of the job (not required | type: str);
        language_code -- language code for the language used in the input mp3
                         file (not required | type: str | default: 'en-US').

    """

    transcribe = boto3.client('transcribe')
    if not job_name:
        job_name = basename(src).replace('.mp3', '')
    if not dst:
        try:
            response = transcribe.start_transcription_job(
                    TranscriptionJobName=job_name, Media={'MediaFileUri': src},
                    MediaFormat='mp3', LanguageCode=language_code,
                    Settings={'ShowSpeakerLabels': True, 'MaxSpeakerLabels': 2})
        except Exception as exception:
            return 1
    else:
        try:
            response = transcribe.start_transcription_job(
                    TranscriptionJobName=job_name, Media={'MediaFileUri': src},
                    MediaFormat='mp3', LanguageCode=language_code,
                    OutputBucketName = dst,
                    Settings={'ShowSpeakerLabels': True, 'MaxSpeakerLabels': 2})
        except Exception as exception:
            return 1
    return response

# Example. Transcribe
# 'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/example.mp3' file
#transcribe_mp3('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/example.mp3')

# Example. Transcribe
# 'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/example.mp3' file to
# 'examplebucket' bucket on AWS S3
#transcribe_mp3('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/example.mp3',
#               'examplebucket')

# Example. Transcribe all mp3 file from
# 'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/examples/' directory
# (recursively) on AWS S3 with AWS Transcribe
#transcribe_dir('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/examples/')
# or
#transcribe_dir('examplebucket/examples/')

# Example. Transcribe all mp3 file from
# 'https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/examples/' directory
# (recursively) on AWS S3 to 'examplebucket' bucket on AWS S3
# with AWS Transcribe
#transcribe_dir('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/examplebucket/examples/',
#               'examplebucket')
# or
#transcribe_dir('examplebucket/examples/', 'examplebucket')
```

issue_title: Python 3 port uses Unicode to represent byte strings
issue_body:
pygit2, when built for Python 3, treats paths as Unicode and will fail if a path isn't decodable as the filesystem encoding. But Git paths are byte strings, not Unicode strings. This includes refs, so repos with branch names containing non-UTF-8 sequences are completely unusable on most systems:

    >>> repo.listall_references()
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
    UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x80 in position 14: invalid start byte

pygit2's behaviour under Python 2 is correct; listall_references and other APIs returns byte strings as they are in the Git model. I don't see why the behaviour should differ by Python version, as both types exist in both languages. It seems to me that the default low-level API should return byte strings to match the underlying model and handle all cases, and convenience wrappers which return Unicode strings could be added if people actually want them. As it stands, some perfectly valid Git repos are unusable except on Python 2.

issue_title: Bug in _create_instantiateVAppTemplateParams
issue_body:
When creating a new machine, _create_instantiateVAppTemplateParams attempts to pass a set of parameters to the templateParameters, using add_SourcedItem. Unfortunately, add_SourcedItem isn't supported in versions older than 5.6, so the whole operation fails.
The snippet below is where things break, on the line
**templateParams.add_SourcedItem(params)**

```python
if ((self.version == "1.0") or (self.version == "1.5") or
           (self.version == "5.1") or (self.version == "5.5")):
            if vm_name or vm_cpus or vm_memory:
                params = vcloudType.SourcedVmInstantiationParamsType()
                params.set_Source(vcloudType.ReferenceType(href=vm_href))
                templateParams.add_SourcedItem(params)
                if vm_name:
                    pass```

issue_title: Payment/Sale Date API Bug
issue_body:
There appears to be an API bug when looking up a Payment by payment ID. The response seems to end up with the current date as the `create_time` and `update_time` fields, including inside of the Sale resource under `related_resources` in `transactions`.

However, if you take the sale ID from inside `related_resources` and lookup the Sale directly, it has the correct `create_time` and `update_time`.

Here's a code example of the bug:

````Python
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> payment = paypalrestsdk.Payment.find("PAY-XXXXX")
>>> nested_sale = payment.transactions[0].related_resources[0].sale
>>> sale = paypalrestsdk.Sale.find(nested_sale.id)
>>> nested_sale.create_time == sale.create_time
False
>>> nested_sale.update_time == sale.update_time
False
>>> nested_sale.create_time                 
u'2016-02-06T15:20:40Z'
>>> datetime.utcnow()  # Current UTC time should be just after the create_time
datetime(2016, 2, 6, 15, 21, 9, 934184)
````

issue_title: Diff incorrectly populates a_blob and b_blob
issue_body:
I was using GitPython to take some diffs and I noticed an inconsistency in behavior when diffing commits against the NULL_TREE. In most cases, the descendant will have it's commits populated in a_blob, but it looks like when diffing against NULL_TREE, the commits end up in the wrong place.

```python
>>> from git import Repo, NULL_TREE
>>> from git.objects import Commit
>>> repo = Repo('../../fun/dummy')
>>> commit = Commit.new(repo, '87efa5a4')
>>> commit.diff(NULL_TREE)[0]
<git.diff.Diff object at 0x101443cb0>
>>> commit.diff(NULL_TREE)[0].a_blob == None
True
>>> commit.diff(NULL_TREE)[0].b_blob
<git.Blob "938e9ca15a7d3083db89c2d99e51024812fdec16">

# The expected behavior is that the descendant's
# blob will be populated in a_blob like below

>>> parent = commit.parents[0]
>>> commit.diff(parent)[0].a_blob
<git.Blob "938e9ca15a7d3083db89c2d99e51024812fdec16">
>>> commit.diff(parent)[0].b_blob == None
True
```

issue_title: Unify namedtuple/dict way samples are treated in Reader implementation
issue_body:
Currently we use use both namedtuple and dictionary interchangeably. This results in a lot of back and forth conversions and makes the code more cumbersome. 

Since nametuple is kinda syntactic suger for Reader users, we should make all internal usage of rows to be a dictionary (since it is easier for code parametrized by field names to handle).

issue_title: How to use no-delete?
issue_body:
As advertised in the docs:

You can choose what happens when you delete an object :
1. it can be masked from your database (soft delete)
2. it can be normally deleted (hard delete)
3. it can be hard-deleted, but if its deletion would delete other objects, it will only be masked
4. it can be never deleted or masked from your database (no delete, use with caution)

I could not find option 4. Could you please clarify, how to implement no-delete with  `django-safedelete`?

Thanks!

issue_title: "classifiers" should be a list
issue_body:
The *classifiers* argument of `setup()` in `setup.py` should be a list. Currently it is a result of `filter()`, i.e. a list in Python 2, but an iterator in Python 3. Maybe there are other incompatibilities with Python 3.

This will cause a problem in Python 3.7. See [bpo-19610](https://bugs.python.org/issue19610) for details.

issue_title: Support verification of GnuPG signed packages
issue_body:
You can upload packages to PyPI and sign the distributions using GnuPG with `twine` (https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#upload-your-distributions).

My recommendation for `pipenv` is that you can (optionally) add a GnuPG key to the piplock file for packages. The update or installation process will then only work if the packages has been signed with the correct GnuPG key.

While I agree that `pipenv` should not add too many features and focus on being relatively lighweight and functional, I think this is an important issue because of security. Manually veryfing signatures is not the way to go.

issue_title: pipenv init subcommand
issue_body:
##### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

When I run `pipenv install <somepackage>` in a directory where the Pipfile doesn't exist yet pipenv automatically creates the `Pipfile`. While this is convenient, sometimes I accidentally run it in a wrong directory. After that happens I have to manually remove both Pipenv & Pipenv.lock.

##### Describe the solution you'd like

To prevent the mistakes like the one described above, I propose this:

1. Introduce a pipenv subcommand `init`:

```
pipenv init     # Create the initial Pipfile and/or Pipfile.lock in the current directory
pipenv install <somepackage>     # Install packages as usual
```

2. Modify the subcommand `inistall` so that it doesn't create Pipfile automatically; if the command is issued and Pipfile doesn't exist in the current directory or in one of the parent directories, print a message and exit. The message may contain a hint that the user should first run `pipenv init`.

Also, there are other well-known tools that work in a similar fashion, for example `git` or `vagrant`.

issue_title: Use multiprocessing.Queue to allow tqdm
"update_security_for" should not be executed recursively
issue_body:
Given a structure of same-kind folderish objects:
```
- A
  - B
    - C
```

When `update_security_for` is called for all objects of a kind, this may be recursive and certain children are updated multiple times, e.g.:

=> A, b, c, B, c, C

This is very inefficient.

We should implement our own `reindexObjectSecurity` as helper function which is less stupid, optimized for our case.

issue_title: Parsing '2018-04-12 17:20:03.12345678999a' raises an exception
issue_body:
To reproduce:
```
import dateparser
dateparser.parse('2018-04-12 17:20:03.12345678999a')
```

On Windows, CPython 3.6.4, and dateparser 0.7.0, this raises OverflowError (Python int too large to convert to C long).
On Linux, CPython 3.6.4, and dateparser 0.7.0, this raises OverflowError (signed integer is less than minimum).
On Linux, Pypy 5.10.1, and dateparser 0.7.0, this raises ValueError ('year must be in 1..9999', -12345676981).

I expected parsing to fail and return None, or, in the future, raise a DateParserError.

```dateparser.parse('2018-04-12 17:20:03.12345678999b')``` succeeds in all above platforms.

issue_body:

       def from_crawler(cls, crawler):
           ext = SpiderCtlExtension()
        
           ext.project_name = crawler.settings.get('BOT_NAME')
           crawler.signals.connect(ext.spider_opened, signal=signals.spider_opened)
           crawler.signals.connect(ext.spider_closed, signal=signals.spider_closed)
        
           return ext
        
       def spider_opened(self, spider):
           sql = """UPDATE ctl_crawler
                    SET status = 'RUNNING'
                    WHERE jobid = '{}'  """.format(os.getenv("SCRAPY_JOB"))
           engine.execute(sql)
        
       def spider_closed(self,spider,reason):
           print "CLOSE SPIDER"
           sql = """UPDATE ctl_crawler
                    SET status = '{}'
                    WHERE jobid = '{}'  """.format(reason.upper(),os.getenv("SCRAPY_JOB"))
           engine.execute(sql)

The spider_opened method gets called, the spider_closed method gets called when the crawl is actually finished. However on a cancel, the method is not called.

Another symptom is that the spider's log ends abruptly, without a log entry for the closing event. After going through the sources, I suspect the culprit is actually the way Twisted handles signals on windows:

http://twistedmatrix.com/trac/browser/tags/releases/twisted-8.0.0/twisted/internet/_dumbwin32proc.py#L245

	    def signalProcess(self, signalID):
	        if self.closed:
	            raise error.ProcessExitedAlready()
	        if signalID in ("INT", "TERM", "KILL"):
	            win32process.TerminateProcess(self.hProcess, 1)

If I understand correctly what is happening, you have the following setup:

* Twisted container (created in scrapyd/launcher.py)
    * Crawler Process (created by scrapy/crawler.py)
        * Twisted container
            * Crawler

The issue here is that the outer Twisted container exits immediately, as also indirectly said here:
https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/issues/1001#issuecomment-68720943

To fix this, it is necessary to somehow trigger a graceful shutdown of the Crawler Process, without terminating the outer container right away.

I've had a hard time replicating this issue.  The systems I'm seeing this happen on is Python 3.5 on Ubuntu 16.04, but it's not reliable: one machine shows it, the other shows it only some of the time.  My best guess is that there's nondeterminism because of dict iteration order, that the process of reading the spec somehow involves the network, or both.

issue_title: hough_line default angle and distance ranges are poorly chosen
issue_body:
The ```theta``` parameter in ```hough_line```defaults to ```linspace(-pi/2, +pi/2, 180)```. This is a poor choice for two reasons:
 1.  -pi/2 and +pi/2 are degenerate
 1. This means there's no point at ```theta=0```

A better choice would be ```linspace(-pi/2, +pi/2, 180, False)```.

The distance range would benefit from the same change so that ```dist=0``` is also included.

Finally, it is possible to specify a custom angle range, but not a custom distance range. Is there a good reason why one can't stipulate a distance range.

issue_title: Check out libsimdpp, it should help supporting multiple platforms
issue_body:
Hi, just noticed this new SIMDified library in development through a Hacker News post. If cross-platform SIMD support is among your objectives, I think you should check out libsimdpp (https://github.com/p12tic/libsimdpp). It allows you to write the SIMD code once, then compile it for different instruction sets, combine the resulting object files into single library and then dispatch depending on the actual CPU type on runtime. For relatively simple code such as image processing such multi-platform support would be straightforward. At the moment libsimdpp already supports the most popular SIMD instruction sets: SSE2 to SSE4.1, XOP, AVX, AVX2, AVX512F, NEON and Altivec, with AVX512BW/DQ, MIPS MSA and POWER7/8 VSX support being in development.

Disclosure: I'm the author of libsimdpp.

issue_title: Fix Windows bug with types
issue_body:
After last merge involving `src/nupic/types/Types.h`, Windows build failed (https://ci.appveyor.com/project/david-ragazzi/nupic-core/build/1.0.11). I found the error is in this block: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/03c21fd51eae9b88c39456772a2a288c7e716cf1/src/nupic/types/Types.h#L193 , which re-define `NTA_Int32` and `NTA_UInt32` on a Windows environment. 

I tried remove this block, and force Windows to re-use the same types defined for `*nix` environments but this fix didn't work on Windows 64bit. The solution was define data types on specific blocks for `windows` and `*nix` tastes, avoiding mix them. Something like this:

```
#if defined(NTA_OS_WINDOWS)
  #if defined(NTA_ARCH_32)
    typedef long NTA_*;
    ...
  #else
    ...
  #endif
#else
  # *nix systems
  #if defined(NTA_ARCH_32)
    ...
  #else
    ...
  #endif
#endif
```

issue_title: default_validity_start and default_ca_validity_end documentation is confusing
issue_body:
Creating Ca/Cert with timzone enabled results in Django warnings: "RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField Ca.validity_start received a naive datetime (2018-01-02 00:00:00) while time zone support is active.
  RuntimeWarning)" warning 
```

This looked like a bug, so I checked the implementation and I find the comment quite unhelpful.
def default_validity_start():
    """
    sets validity_start field to 1 day before the current date
    (avoids "certificate not valid yet" edge case)
    intentionally returns naive datetime (not timezone aware)
    """
   ...
```

Well, I see that we return naive date, that's what this 2-line function does. Re-using this code I'm wondering WHY we do this. My first reaction was "i'll fix it", but now I'm not sure what can of worms is hiding behind such change.

The hipster punctuation doesn't help understanding the intent too.

Please, please, please clarify the function documentation providing hints for end-users regarding this behavior.

issue_title: Segmentation fault
issue_body:
The last few days, my CI tests fail with a **Segmentation fault (core dumped)** error when trying to use django-x509. I noticed that the CI tests of django-x509 fail as well with the same error. I was able to track it down to the latest cryptography release.

Django-x509 requires pyopenssl, which requires cryptography. The latest release of cryptography [2.1.3](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/releases/tag/2.1.3) was published a few days ago. The changelog contains a single entry:

>Updated Windows, macOS, and manylinux1 wheels to be compiled with OpenSSL 1.1.0g.

I was able to work around the segmentation fault by pinning cryptography to 2.1.2. Perhaps django-x509 should also be pinning cryptography, until a proper fix is implemented that allows upgrading cryptography without segfaults.

issue_title: RFC commit message style and release notes
issue_body:
### About

This RFC summarises reasons behind [git commit log message style](http://invenio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/technology/git.html#r2-remarks-on-commit-log-messages) that
was born in the Invenio 1 (and 2) monolithic world. Most of the original reasons
do not apply in the Invenio 3 independent package world anymore. Now that we are
approaching the first Invenio 3 stable release, the time is perfect to reflect
on the existing practices and how to improve them.

**Note:** This will be the topic of an [Invenio Developer Forum](https://indico.cern.ch/category/6046/) 
live discussions, so even though every one is encouraged to comment in here, it would
be great to meet up and reflect on the matter IRL together.

### 1. General upstream conventions

Upstream git conventions talk about a commit log headline, followed by a blank
line, followed by the multi-line body. Just as we use PEP-8 for Python code, we
use these upstream general git conventions for commit log messages. (Users of
Emacs, Vim and possibly other code editors have a "commit log mode" that helps
to format the messages according to these general conventions.) This RFC is not
questioning these; it focuses solely on the Invenio-specific semantics expressed
inside.

### 2. Commit headline format

In Invenio 1 (and 2), the convention was to use:

    component: brief description

permitting to rapidly distinguish commits to various modules:

    BibFormat: initial release of mobile app formats
    WebSearch: display number of hits in mobile output
    BibDocFile: improve BibDoc display in Files tab

In Invenio 3, the modules have been split into independent packages, making the
original intent not applicable.

Do we consider the separate leading component as still useful? If so, do we use
it to denote component of a package (docs, test, ui, core, api, etc) or nature
of the commit (bug, feature, enhancement, etc) or something else? If not, do we
prefer the headline to be a simple free style phrase? Do we pay attention to
headlines while bisecting?

### 3. Commit signatures

In Invenio 1 (and 2), we have been using Trac and Jenkins, the commit signatures
expressed the commit kwalitee and review status:

    Signed-off-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
    Reviewed-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
    Reported-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>
    Co-authored-by: Tibor Simko <tibor.simko@cern.ch>

In Invenio 3, we are using GitHub where centralised reviews happen, and Travis
where commit quality and CI tests are enforced.

Do we keep some of the signatures to express credit for reporting and/or
co-authoring? Do we remove all signatures?

### 4. Release notes vs commit notes

In Invenio 1 (and 2), the point releases were rare, and services such as INSPIRE
often used ``master`` branch in production, and wanted to know what changed
precisely between any two given ``SHA1a`` and ``SHA1b``. Hence the release notes
that were compiled "live" for any commit log message range:

    kwalitee prepare release -c sha1a..sha1b

In Invenio 3, the point releases of various packages happen very frequently and
services are often deploying from PyPI releases only. (Side note: pinning
specific versions that are "waiting" for release due to some blockers.)

Do we consider it useful to keep relying on git log based messages to compile
release notes? Do we consider it sufficient to maintain a separate RELEASE-NOTES
file dissociated from commit logs? Do we consider it useful to be able to
compile changes automatically at any point in time? Do we compile them only at
the release time? Do we pay attention to release notes vs commit notes while
bisecting?

### 5. Release note classification

In Invenio 1, the release note messages were originally compiled from short
commit log headlines, see for example [1.1.0 release notes](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/releases/tag/v1.1.0).
This was often too brief to be understandable by the users.

This led to the introduction of commit log labels such as NEW, BETTER, FIX in
front of longer sentences, so that the release notes would be much more
self-understood and and categorised. See for example [1.2.1 release notes](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/releases/tag/v1.2.1).

Do we want to keep or amend the NEW/FIX/BETTER/SECURITY/etc release note
classification?

### 6. Release note author

In Invenio 1 (and 2), the release notes were created from the commit log
messages via commands like:

     kwalitee prepare release -c v1.1.6..

Hence it was important to use proper English phrases in the commit log messages.
Oftentimes developers didn't pay attention to this, which necessitated (i)
cherry-picking by the maintainer and/or (ii) rebasing by the developer.

Do we want the release note messages to be written by the contributors or by the
maintainers? Do we let maintainers rewrite the release notes either by
cherry-picking, or by using explicit merge commits, or by maintaining a separate
file, or by amending GitHub issue body, etc? Is the PR author the best person to
think of the release message? Is it the maintainer?

### 7. Body format

In Invenio 1 (and 2), the convention was to use itemised list:

    * BETTER Improves foo.

    * FIX Fixes bar.

permitting to express different facets of what the given commit does. (For
example, enhances a feature and fixes a related bug at the same time.)

Do we consider this still useful? Do we require one commit to announce one thing
and one thing only?

### 8. Issue integrations

In Invenio 1 (and 2), the link between commits, PRs, and issues were expressed
via usual directives:

     (addresses #1234)
     (closes #1234)

The format was parenthesised so that release notes could be properly compiled.
Some developers didn't use this format, requiring usual amendment cycle. Are we
happy with the practices? Are we remove the constraints?

### 9. Message format checks

If we retain using some structured commit message format, it should be easy to 
check and plug into Travis CI. Currently, we use:

    kwalitee check message master..upstream/pr/13

and this is plugged into CI for several repositories. Do developers run these locally? 
Do we plug these into Travis CI everywhere?

### Proposal time!

The above points summarised the main reasons behind current practices. Many of
the reasons are no longer valid in the world of Invenio 3 distributed packages.
Now that we are about to release the first Invenio 3 "production" packages,
the time is good to ponder and improve the system.

The high-level goals for selecting a consistent commit note system for Invenio
packages:

- the commits should be atomic etc (as usual);
- the commit message should describe changes fully, so that it would be
  self-understood what the commit does, even without reading the code;
- the commit notes should facilitate later bisecting;
- the release notes should be easily maintainable and not prone to getting 
  forgotten or getting out of sync with the code;
- the release notes should be informative to the users.

Let's discuss any finer points and any proposals live at an [Invenio Developer Forum](https://indico.cern.ch/category/6046/).

CC @inveniosoftware/developers

issue_title: WebSubmit: apply wash_for_xml() to form fields
issue_body:
Originally on 2013-02-14

Currently, `wash_for_xml()` is implicitly called before creating the MARCXML representation of a record from the just-submitted metadata. 
This is useful in order to prevent bad situation where the XML is not valid at all.

However there are other situations where it would be nice to still strip away "bad" UTF-8 characters (e.g. when the raw values are passed to the LaTeX stamping function).

A radical solution to this issue would be to wash for bad characters in the early stages of submission, when the submitted form is parsed and is about to be written to disk, i.e. in `websubmit_engine.interface()` and `websubmit_engine.endaction()`.

issue_title: App crashes every time Elasticsearch returns an error
issue_body:
Everytime Elasticsearch returns an error (e.g. there is some non-escaped forward slash in the query which is not accepted in out of the box configuration) the return value is not a JSON but a Java Traceback.

In troubleshooting there is a note for these cases: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/troubleshooting/issues/24

Would it be better to treat non-escaped values in the application before sending to Elasticsearch? Or Capture the ``ValueError`` exception and just log it and return error or empty json?

From my point of view the second option is more flexible to allow custom ES configurations.

ES Reserved Characters [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/query-dsl-query-string-query.html#_reserved_characters)

PS: This also applies when searching, therefore I am not sure if it applies more to Invenio-Search or even Invenio-Records-Rest

issue_title: zip: use the `allowZip64` parameter for files over 4GB
issue_body: Preview of Zipfiles larger that 2GB is not supported at the moment. Passing the `allowZip64` flag, would make this possible, though it's not clear from Python's [`zipfile` docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/zipfile.html#zipfile.ZipFile) if there are complications.

issue_title: SPIRES query "find foo witten"
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-08-24

Currently, when users use SPIRES search syntax, find foo witten is being interpreted as a Boolean search for the word foo and the word witten when foo was not recognised as being one of valid Originally on 2012-08-24

Currently, when users use SPIRES search syntax, `find foo witten` is being interpreted as a Boolean search for the word `foo` and the word `witten` when `foo` was not recognised as being one of valid SPIRES syntax operators.

When one typed `find foo witten` into SPIRES, the system replied_Your search term was not valid for this database. Please try your search again._ which was much more reasonable behaviour.

We may want to fix this on INSPIRE too, otherwise it may be confusion-prone to see some hits  when `foo` matches a small subset of real records.

issue_title: `fields.Path` doesn't handle home directories
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.configuration/+bug/494493, @batlock666 reported:

> The fieldtype Path from zope.configuration.fields doesn't handle home directories, indicated by a ~ in the path. The path ~/some.file will be interpreted as /path/to/some/package/~/some.file, while it should be something like /home/someuser/some.file.

@freddrake followed up:

> How much need is there for ~/some.file or ~user/some.file in ZCML?
>
> If you're not using this from ZCML, could you describe you use case?

@batlock666 replied:

> I use it in a custom ZCML-directive. The directive defines a configuration-file in INI-format. The handler for the directive reads the file, and makes the contents available in a global utility:
> 
> In ZCML, I define:
```xml
<ugent:configurationFile path="~/some.config.ini"
                         name="some_config" />
```
> Then in Zope, I can do this:
> 
```python
config = getUtility(IConfigurationFile, name="some_config")
print config.getSections()
['some_section', 'some_other_section']
```
>
> I use this to define connection-settings for MySQL and LDAP.

issue_title: Preserving structure when re-saving Keras HDF5 files
issue_body:
In some cases, opening an HDF5 file, editing it, and then re-saving it, can cause detrimental changes to the format. Something that was an attribute might have turned into a group, or vice versa. It would also be nice if it preserved the compression choices of the original file.

I have noticed problems with this for instance with a [Keras](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/) model saved to HDF5.

This is not a trivial problem to solve and requires storing meta data that may give clues to how it should be saved. It can also be addressed by allowing edits without a full load and re-save, which relates to #22.

issue_title: API:  breaking apart  factor_data into fwd returns and factor value/quantile
issue_body:
I am copying/pasting here the comment from @ssanderson (from [here](https://github.com/quantopian/alphalens/issues/253#issuecomment-364850076)) which requires a dedicated discussion thread

> As an aside, in the long term I'm not sure I'm convinced we need combining logic at all. I commented in #258 (comment) that the current factor_data layout forces a lot of duplication if you want to analyze multiple factors.
> 
> Depending on how important a use-case it is to analyze multiple factors (and it seems like the desire to efficiently analyze multiple factors is one of the motiviating ideas for this thread), I think we might want to consider breaking apart the monolithic factor_data format into returns + group columns (which can be shared across multiple analyses) and factor-specific factor value/quantile columns . That's a much larger API change than what we've been discussing so far though, so it probably deserves its own discussion thread.

issue_title: Logging from celery worker processes doesn't work under some circumstances
issue_body:
This refers only to the **Threaded transport**.

I use Celery and Raven in my Django project. Project uses some third party libraries that are somewhat outdated and output warnings about some deprecations on initialization.

Also I have enabled WARNING level for sentry log hander.

This results in following failure scenario:

1. Celery worker starts (`celery worker -A myproject`)
2. Django initializes models and other stuff
3. Anything is being send into raven (in my case its deprecation warning, but it can be something more important)
4. Raven initializes threaded transport, which starts its thread.
5. ....
6. Celery forks worker processes
7. Worker processes adopt everything from parent celery worker, except threads.
8. Raven in worker process tries to send something to its thread, but the thread doesn't exist and message stays in the queue.

I have found a workaround for this: attach a handler to worker_init Celery's signal and reinitialize all transports there:

```py
from celery.signals import worker_process_init

@worker_process_init.connect
def reset_raven_transport_registry(sender, **kwargs):
    from raven.base import Client, TransportRegistry, default_transports
    Client._registry = TransportRegistry(transports=default_transports)
```

I don't know whose issue is it and I don't know would you fix it or not, I just want to add a solution if someone will encounter this problem, this will save a couple hours of debugging.

issue_title: Ditch the "CDN" approach using JavaScript
issue_body:
This feels like a really dirty trick, getting rid of the CSS-only version. There's *no* reason users should run arbitrary JavaScript just for some CSS, but you're almost forcing them to by making less-experienced developers go down the path of using JavaScript to use Font Awesome. And let's be honest here - it's not to serve the users, it's to associate e-mail addresses with web sites, that's what you get when you generate unique embed codes and require an e-mail address to do so.

The original CSS should be the first option. THEN the "custom build" JS option. Maybe. But that option feels like a dirty, dirty trick to me, and you just lost my trust by dropping the change with no fanfare and a hand-wavy "hey buddy, this is better".

issue_title: Multiple Patronis managing a single PostgreSQL instance
issue_body: We should make some kind of lock file in the PostgreSQL data directory, that would indicate that the instance is managed by Patroni of a given cluster, in order to prevent multiple Patroni daemons to attach to a single data directory. Right now this is possible, if you run a second patroni with the same data_dir, but point it to another cluster, both would compete to set the instance into the desired state (master, replica) and would likely render the instance unusable.

I'm trying to figure out how to page through results.

Create the object:

```python
dg = ckanapi.RemoteCKAN('http://catalog.data.gov', apikey=_API_KEY)
```

Execute query:

```python
r = dg.action.package_search(q='fish')
```

This gives me the first ten results. However, there doesn't appear to be a way to get the *next* ten results. I get the exact same results whether I call the last command, or:

```python
dg.action.package_search(q='fish', offset=20)
```

or

```python
dg.action.package_search(q='fish', XYZ=20)
```

They all return successful.

issue_title: Pyral only allows for keying off the workspace by string name
issue_body: Pyral will only allow you to set your workspace by string name.  It does not matter whether you do it when creating a new Rally connection or later with rally.setWorkspace.  Because Rally allows you to have multiple workspaces with the same name you would need to key off of the ref to make sure you are hitting the correct workspace.

In my systems terminal, the prompt itself has a different color from the text. This is not the case for guake, even when the color settings are explicitly set to be equal to the system terminals. I found this to be a very nice visual guide to identify points in which I have issued commands when scrolling through a lot of output.

![screenshot from 2016-04-06 14-16-34](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9695626/14316376/eb793818-fc02-11e5-932e-469fa8dc233c.png)

issue_title: Feature request - alert sound in guake .
issue_body:
In guake I cannot hear an alert sound which I can hear in
gnome-terminal or chromium. The test-case is go to either the location
bar in chromium and hit backspace (each time the alert sound will be
played). Same or similar experience can be shared in gnome-terminal.
You hit backspace at the $ prompt itself and the alert sound will be
played.This can also be seen in editors like leafpad when they reach
the end or beginning of the document and go no further. This is in
gnome-flashback.

Having such alert sounds helps whenever you are absent-minded or feeling sleepy and working on the terminal.

issue_title: Un-fork this forked repo
issue_body:
It appears we're in a situation where a fork (irods/python-irodsclient) has now become "upstream", and the original upstream repo (iplantcollaborativeopensource/python-irodsclient) is no longer maintained. Aside from being unintuitive, this is a usability issue as GitHub doesn't let you use search code inside a fork.

I suggest the maintainers do something [like this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/29326767/unfork-a-github-fork-without-deleting). Also, perhaps iplantcollaborativeopensource/python-irodsclient should be deprecated if it's no longer maintained.

issue_body:
This is what happens if you call with a incorrect stored query name
```
>>> res_q = SpecificQuery(SESS, alias="ThisQueryDoesntExist")
>>> res_q.get_results()
<generator object get_results at 0x18a43c0>
>>> for r in res_q.get_results():
...     pprint.pprint(r)
... 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/iRODS/sanger-scripts/virtualenv_prc_0.6.0/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/irods/query.py", line 334, in get_results
    for result_set in self.get_batches():

    result_set = self.execute()
  File "/usr/local/iRODS/sanger-scripts/virtualenv_prc_0.6.0/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/irods/query.py", line 314, in execute
    response = conn.recv()
  File "/usr/local/iRODS/sanger-scripts/virtualenv_prc_0.6.0/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/irods/connection.py", line 70, in recv
    raise get_exception_by_code(msg.int_info)

issue_title: Expose the count of calls to each endpoint
issue_body:
Add an endpoint called `/stats` that returns an object where each property is the name of an endpoint, and the value is a `Number` representing the count of calls for the given endpoint in the last `days` days. `days` is passed as a query parameter and defaults to `30`.

This is valid for both the flask and the current API.

issue_title: Prepare cartoframes.carto.com for examples methods
issue_body:
Example datasets are soon coming to cartoframes. The code (https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/pull/382) has mostly been written but there are a couple of tasks which need to be completed before this is merged in.

- [x] Get cartoframes-testing ready - see #419 
  * Testing / CI needs to be moved to another account (`cartoframes-testing.carto.com`). 
- [x] `cartoframes.carto.com` needs to be curated with new datasets. These datasets come from examples, guides, documentation, etc. We can also host some that are used for crankshaft functions (e.g., moran's i, kmeans). See some tickets about that: https://github.com/cartodb/cartoframes/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3Adocumentation
- [x] Old testing datasets removed from cartoframes acct
- [x] Update example notebooks as well as docs to point out new examples utilties
- [x] Update guides: see #432 
- [x] `cartoframes.carto.com` needs to be provisioned with all the named maps template to create maps off of the example datasets. we could limit map creation to one or two layers to make this easier by overriding the function a bit.
- [x] Auth API needs to be on all user accounts

issue_title: Support impersonation
issue_body:
I would like to see support for impersonation so that I can use pycontribs/jira to write trusted applications.

With the right credentials, and having the right kind of application link configured, it is possible to have an application impersonate registered users.

https://answers.atlassian.com/questions/247528/how-do-you-impersonate-a-user-with-jira-oauth
https://answers.atlassian.com/questions/263719/using-oauth-and-the-jira-java-rest-client

issue_title: Support new ability to suppress notifications on update
issue_body: In [JRA-34423](https://jira.atlassian.com/browse/JRA-34423), Atlassian added the ability to suppress notifications on a per-update basis.  The implementation appears to be via a query string parameter (notifyUsers=false) on the update POST, per [the docs](https://docs.atlassian.com/jira/REST/cloud/#api/2/issue-editIssue).  It would be great if we could toggle that switch via the issue.update() method.

issue_title: Process crashes if rendered data name is too long
issue_body:
After process is done, names of all child objects are re-rendered. If any of the names is longer than 100 characters (limit specified in name field), process crashes.

Names of data object should probably be trimmed to that limit. Increasing of the limit should also be considered (in combination with trimming).

issue_title: Datastore query limits are silently truncated by the v1beta3 API
issue_body:
This bug manifests itself in a couple of "fun" ways:

```python
len(list(client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=300)))
```

Will yield `300`, assuming there are `300` entities of that kind.

```python
len(list(client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=500)))
```

Assuming there are `500` entities of that kind, will yield `500`. If there are `n` entities where `n > 500`, it will yield `n`.

This all seems to be caused by the `v1beta3` api truncating the limits to `300`:

```python
entities, more, cursor = client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=500).next_page()
```

`len(entities)` will be `300` and _not_ `500` even if there are `500` or more entities of that kind. 

We're currently using the following snippet to work around this issue:

```python

def _all_entities(iterator):
    entities, cursor = [], None
    while iterator._limit > 0:
        xs, more, cursor = iterator.next_page()
        iterator._limit -= len(xs)
        entities.extend(xs)
        if not more:
            break

    return entities, cursor
```

issue_title: Options for configuring 'LogSink.destination'.
issue_body:
When I drafted the [logging usage doc](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/logging-api/docs/logging-usage.rst), I assumed that users would want to pass in a string identifying the bucket, bigquery dataset, or pubsub topic, to which log entries would be exported, and I guessed at three properties, `storage_bucket`, `bigquery_dataset`, and `pubsub_topic`, which would consume and validate those strings.

Now, while working on the implementation, I believe that it might be better to have a `destination` property, whose setter would be willing to take any of:

- a string (it would do no validation, but just store it).
- a `Bucket` instance
- a `Dataset` instance
- a `Topic` instance.

In the case of the last three, the `destination` property setter would synthesize the appropriate destination string and store it.

We would then be able to accept a `destination` argument to `Sink.__init__` and `Client.sink`, which would just rely on the `destination` property setter.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Support for GCS Cloud Pub/Sub Notifications
issue_body:
GCS has a new feature (currently in private alpha) wherein buckets can be configured to send object change notifications to Cloud Pub/Sub topics, rather than as push notifications to HTTPS endpoints.

For gcloud-python, I imagine such a feature might look like this:

    from gcloud import pubsub
    from gcloud import storage

    storage_client = storage.Client()
    pubsub_client = pubsub.Client()
    bucket = storage_client.get_bucket(BUCKET_ID)
    topic = pubsub_client.topic(TOPIC_ID)
    notification_config = bucket.send_notifications_to(topic)

issue_title: Logging:  deadlock when using the subprocess module after cloud logging init
issue_body:
Repro instructions:

Within a `docker run -it ubuntu`, run:

```
apt-get update
apt-get -y install python3-dev python3-dbg gdb python3-pip
pip3 install google-cloud

cat << EOF > /tmp/repro.py
import logging
import subprocess

from google.cloud import logging as cloud_logging
import google.cloud.logging.handlers as cloud_handlers


logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
client = cloud_logging.Client()
handler = cloud_handlers.CloudLoggingHandler(client, name="repro-test")
cloud_handlers.setup_logging(handler)

for i in range(100):
    print("[%d] subprocess check_call..." % i)
    subprocess.check_call("true")

EOF

while [[ true ]]; do python3 /tmp/repro.py ; sleep 0.2 ; echo ; done
```

I tried to minimize the repro from a much larger production script.  Removing some of the included steps (e.g. the basic config call) will prevent this from occurring in my experience (though that may just be timing induced).

Once the `python3 /tmp/repro.py` loop runs, it will typically hangs at:
```

issue_title: Dill fails to pickle class instance defined in function scope with super() call in __init__.
issue_body:
Here is the code demonstrating problem:
```
import dill

def main():
    class A(object): pass
    class B(A):
        def __init__(self): super(B, self).__init__()
        def do(self): return 5

    dill.dumps(B())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```


which fails with `RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object`.

I found out that `B` class is added to `__init__` closure (it seems logical, since B type is defined only in `main` function and proxy object returned by `super` may need this type). So, there is a cycle in dump process:
`B -> B dict -> __init__ function -> __init__ function closure -> B`

This can be fixed in code above, for example, using `A.__init__(self)` instead of `super()` call.

I suppose this can be fixed in dill by dropping class from method closure if method is defined in the class with special bool flag that function should contain this class in closure. On unpickling the original class can be added back to closure depending on this flag. But I don't feel to have enough expertise in the question :)

issue_title: "Ignore Hosts" support
issue_body:
Hi clowwindy,

Can you please add support for "Ignore Hosts" in sslocal? 
Just like the ones in the Linux system network proxy preferences.

I am asking for this feature because we need to use the shadowsocks' socks5 proxy in some software directly rather than the system configured proxy, but we want some network traffics (especially  localhost) can get bypassed in the socks proxy. 

To get it clearer, here is my use case:
My goal: send emails to an SMTP server in my Java program.
My problem: the SMTP protocol is blocked by the firewall.
My solutions: use socks proxy to bypass the firewall, so I will run my program with `java -DsocksProxyHost=127.0.0.1 -DsocksProxyPort=9876 ...`.
New problem: all TCP connections will go through the socks proxy, even mysql (jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/xxx) and postgresql (jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/yyy) requests, then my program fails.

It would be awesome if we could support setting ignore hosts in the conf file in shadowsocks.

Reference:
[How do I configure JavaMail to work through my proxy server? ](http://www.oracle.com/technetwork/java/javamail/faq/index.html#proxy)
It's said that I can set [`mail.smtp.socks.host` and `mail.smtp.socks.port`](https://javamail.java.net/nonav/docs/api/com/sun/mail/smtp/package-summary.html) in my java program. But apparently [org.apache.commons.mail](https://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-email/) does not support it. 

Many thanks,
Huang Jian

issue_title: Warn/error for unmatched glob patterns
issue_body:
There has been a long-standing TODO to warn or error (depending on an option) for unmatched globs, and since we now control the implementation of glob matching, we no longer have a great excuse not to. This would also help make the proposal from #5427 less error prone.

The implementation would be to warn/error for each entry (whether pattern or literal) which did not match anything. This would mean that you would get a warning/error for a glob like `*.scala` that didn't catch any files, and also for `Mispeeled.scala` which indicated a non-existent file.

issue_title: Move local process execution tempdirs into the workdir, add option to not delete them
issue_body:
When executing processes locally, we currently make a temporary directory: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/d0aeac5cb50de06db253f566ad26eb5cc19a8da3/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/local.rs#L79-L86

We should pass the workdir where we make the `local::CommandRunner` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/d0aeac5cb50de06db253f566ad26eb5cc19a8da3/src/rust/engine/src/context.rs#L94-L97) and add it as a new field.

Then we should switch from calling `tempdir()` to calling `tempdir_in` (https://docs.rs/tempfile/3.0.2/tempfile/struct.Builder.html#method.tempdir_in) in a directory under the workdir

Then we should add an boolean flag along the lines of `--delete-local-process-execution-directories` which defaults to True (see https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5932 for how we pass flags), and if it's set to False, not delete the temporary directory. This can be achieved by calling `into_path()` on the `TempDir`: https://docs.rs/tempfile/3.0.2/tempfile/struct.TempDir.html#method.into_path

issue_title: Junit test fails with parallel-threads and the output says it ran twice.
issue_body:
This is with vanilla pants 1.0.0 and so far only triggers on Linux CI - but it is non-deterministic so the platform could be a red-herring.

We have a test that makes db calls. It fails non-deterministically unless parallel-threads is set to 1. When it fails, the output says that the test is being run twice. The circumstantial evidence is that the test is being sharded within the test itself, but I don't really know what these runners do under the hood atm.


The test is open source, so:

        @Test
        def testBulkInsertOne: Unit = {
          val venues = (1 to 5).map(_ => baseTestVenue())
          val venueIds = venues.map(_.id)
          val clauses = venues.map(v => BulkInsertOne(v))
          db.bulk(clauses)

          db.count(Q(ThriftVenue).where(_.id in venueIds)) must_== venues.length
        }

And the results of running `./pants test test::`:


                   There were 2 failures:
                     1) testBulkInsertOne(io.fsq.rogue.spindle.test.EndToEndTest)
                     java.lang.Exception: '1' is not equal to '5'
                     ...
                     2) testBulkInsertOne(io.fsq.rogue.spindle.test.EndToEndTest)
                     java.lang.Exception: '4' is not equal to '0'

There are no other tests with that name and this test is not depended upon by any other targets. If I dig into the `junit_run.py`, it looks to me like the runner is gathering all of the tests into a single batch, but that the test is being broken up into its composite functions.

I see these elements if I examine the `batch` variable on [line 338 of `junit_run.py`](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1.0.x/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/junit_run.py#L338) (which has changed since 1.0.0)

The output is suspicious in the same way, in that it is failing to find 5 entries in one check and is instead finding 1 element and then 4 elements in two.

issue_title: If two jar dependencies are for same org/name, but different classifier, ivy_utils will only pick one
issue_body:
Jin Feng <jinfeng@twitter.com>
12:23 PM (58 minutes ago)

to pants-devel 
Hi,

I'm seeing a problem as follows:

1. I have a 3rdparty BUILD file like:

```
jar_library(name='hbase',
  jars = [
    jar(org='org.apache.hbase', name='hbase', rev=HBASE_REV)
      .intransitive(),
  ]
)

jar_library(name='hbase_tests',
  jars = [
    jar(org='org.apache.hbase', name='hbase', rev=HBASE_REV)
      .with_artifact(classifier='tests')
      .intransitive(),
    # or jar(org='org.apache.hbase', name='hbase', rev=HBASE_REV, classifier='tests').intransitive()
    # the resulting error is the same.
  ]
)
```

2. Now in my junit_tests target, I have:

```
    pants('3rdparty/jvm/org/apache/hbase:hbase'),
    pants('3rdparty/jvm/org/apache/hbase:hbase_tests'),
```

3. The symptom is that after ivy resolve, only the hbase main artifact is resolved and downloaded.

4. I've traced down seems the issue is here https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/ivy_utils.py#L231:

```
    def add_jar(jar):
      coordinate = (jar.org, jar.name)
      existing = jars.get(coordinate)
      jars[coordinate] = jar if not existing else (
        self._resolve_conflict(existing=existing, proposed=jar)
      )
```

Both jars are passed in here (the main one first, the 'tests' classifier one second). However the dict key is org/name, thus no matter what _resolve_conflict do, one will get dropped. In my case, the classifier 'tests' one is dropped. Thus later compilation failed.

5. Wondering what the right fix should be? A couple options

1) in the add_jar method, change to

```
     coordinate = jar.cache_key() # which includes jar.artifacts

    +

   jar_library(name='hbase_tests',
     jars = [
       jar(org='org.apache.hbase', name='hbase', rev=HBASE_REV)
         .with_artifact(classifier=None)
         .with_artifact(classifier='tests')
         .intransitive(),
     ]
   )
```
   The "with_artifact(classifier=None)" line is needed, because if not, the ivy.xml will two sections, one section looks like;

```
    <dependency org="org.apache.hbase"
                name="hbase"
                rev="0.94.t1"                
                transitive="false"
    >
      <conf name="default" mapped="default"/>
    </dependency>
```
And another looks like:

```
    <dependency org="org.apache.hbase"
                name="hbase"
                rev="0.94.t1"
                transitive="false"
    >
      <conf name="default" mapped="default"/>
      <artifact
        name="hbase"
        type="jar"
        m:classifier="tests"        
      />
    </dependency>
```

   And Ivy seems doing some deduping there and only download the second section. By adding classifier=None, the main jar will be added to the second section as well, so both jar's will be downloaded.

2) option 1 puts an unfair burden on people knowing they must add 'classifier=None" there otherwise the main jar wouldn't be downloaded. Seems to me a more sound approach would be doing some sort of *merging*, 
  - either at ivy_utils.py::add_jar level, where two jar are same, but merge their artifacts, or
  - at ivy.xml generation time to merge the same sections together.

3) Or I entirely miss something....

Please advise.

issue_title: Gunicorn/multiprocess with restarting workers, .db files are not cleaned up
issue_body:
We are using Gunicorn with multiple workers with the Gunicorn `max_requests` option. We found that memory usage in our containers increases with every worker restart. By reducing the `max_requests` value so workers restart very frequently, the problem became apparent. 

Using the method described in the readme:

```py
from prometheus_client import multiprocess

def child_exit(server, worker):
    multiprocess.mark_process_dead(worker.pid)
```

While running a performance test, I see the following in the container:
```bash
[root@mock-adapter-8568fdcf6d-lz8tx app]# cd /app/prometheus_tmp
[root@mock-adapter-8568fdcf6d-lz8tx prometheus_tmp]# ls
counter_10.db   counter_214.db  counter_319.db  counter_41.db   counter_91.db     histogram_196.db  histogram_296.db  histogram_401.db  histogram_69.db

counter_130.db  counter_23.db   counter_338.db  counter_444.db  histogram_10.db   histogram_214.db  histogram_319.db  histogram_41.db   histogram_91.db
counter_138.db  counter_244.db  counter_345.db  counter_450.db  histogram_111.db  histogram_222.db  histogram_31.db   histogram_425.db  histogram_97.db
counter_148.db  counter_251.db  counter_353.db  counter_458.db  histogram_117.db  histogram_232.db  histogram_328.db  histogram_433.db  histogram_9.db
counter_154.db  counter_258.db  counter_361.db  counter_470.db  histogram_130.db  histogram_23.db   histogram_338.db  histogram_444.db
counter_175.db  counter_269.db  counter_36.db   counter_48.db   histogram_138.db  histogram_244.db  histogram_345.db  histogram_450.db
counter_187.db  counter_277.db  counter_374.db  counter_56.db   histogram_148.db  histogram_251.db  histogram_353.db  histogram_458.db
counter_18.db   counter_286.db  counter_384.db  counter_61.db   histogram_154.db  histogram_258.db  histogram_361.db  histogram_470.db
counter_196.db  counter_296.db  counter_401.db  counter_69.db   histogram_175.db  histogram_269.db  histogram_36.db   histogram_48.db
counter_1.db    counter_305.db  counter_409.db  counter_77.db   histogram_187.db  histogram_277.db  histogram_374.db  histogram_56.db
counter_202.db  counter_311.db  counter_419.db  counter_84.db   histogram_18.db   histogram_286.db  histogram_384.db  histogram_61.db
```

Watching that directory, new files are created with every new worker and old ones are not being cleaned up. Deleting the files in that directory reduce down the memory usage on that container, which then starts building again.

What is the proper way of dealing with these database files? Is this `mark_process_dead`'s responsibility?

And a general question about Prometheus: do we need to keep metrics around after they are collected? Could we wipe our metrics after the collector hits our metrics endpoint? If so, can this be done via the prometheus_client?

issue_title: After upgrade to v2.3.0, Ffmpeg process for webcam does not terminate (Python 2.7)
issue_body:
Description
-----------

After upgrading from imageio v2.2.0 to v2.3.0 (on a Windows system with Python 2.7 environment), it appears that the ffmpeg process is no longer terminated properly after closing a webcam Reader instance.

How to reproduce issue
------------------------

Here's a minimal test:

```python
import psutil
import imageio

def ffmpeg_alive():
    """ enumerate ffmpeg processes, then wait for them to terminate """
    ffmpeg_processes = []
    for process in psutil.process_iter():
        try:
            if 'ffmpeg' in process.name().lower():
                ffmpeg_processes.append(process)
        except psutil.NoSuchProcess as e:
            print e.message
    still_alive = False
    if ffmpeg_processes:
        __, still_alive = psutil.wait_procs(ffmpeg_processes, timeout=1)
    return still_alive


def test_webcam_ffmpeg_process_termination():
    """ open reader for a webcam, using context manager, then check ffmpeg process status """
    with imageio.get_reader('<video0>') as reader:
        assert reader._proc is not None
        assert ffmpeg_alive()
    assert reader._proc is None
    assert not ffmpeg_alive()


# Run test
print 'imageio version {}'.format(imageio.__version__)
test_webcam_ffmpeg_process_termination()
```

On my system this test *passes* when using a clean conda env running python 2.7 and imageio v2.2.0, but it *fails* when running an (otherwise identical) conda env with imageio v2.3.0 (environment details below). 

Probable cause
---------------

The culprit appears to be the `shell=ISWIN` argument in the `sp.Popen` call in `ffmpeg.Reader._initialize`. 

Proposed solution
-------------------
Quick fix for my specific case would be to remove the `shell` argument (i.e. revert that line to v2.2.0). However, the argument was probably added with a reason? 

Otherwise, a better option would probably be to modify the `ffmpeg.Reader._terminate` method, replacing `self._proc.kill()` by something like the following:

```python
if self.request._video:
    self._proc.communicate('q')
else:
    self._proc.kill()
```

At least this works on my system.

Environment details
--------------------

v2.2.0 environment:

```
certifi                   2016.2.28                py27_0
imageio                   2.2.0                     <pip>
numpy                     1.14.3                    <pip>
Pillow                    5.1.0                     <pip>
pip                       9.0.1                    py27_1
psutil                    5.2.2                    py27_0
python                    2.7.13                        1
setuptools                36.4.0                   py27_1
vc                        9                             0
vs2008_runtime            9.00.30729.5054               0
wheel                     0.29.0                   py27_0
wincertstore              0.2                      py27_0

```

v2.3.0 environment:

```
certifi                   2016.2.28                py27_0
imageio                   2.3.0                     <pip>
numpy                     1.14.3                    <pip>
Pillow                    5.1.0                     <pip>
pip                       9.0.1                    py27_1
psutil                    5.2.2                    py27_0
python                    2.7.13                        1
setuptools                36.4.0                   py27_1
vc                        9                             0
vs2008_runtime            9.00.30729.5054               0
wheel                     0.29.0                   py27_0
wincertstore              0.2                      py27_0

```

System is Windows 10 64 bit, using PyCharm professional 2018.1. Not sure if it matters, but for legacy reasons we are using Miniconda 32-bit.

issue_title: search parameter validation
issue_body:
The PyMISP [search functionality](https://github.com/MISP/PyMISP/blob/e39d014ddca875518a810bc0c2de7be845b6c99b/pymisp/api.py#L893) does not validate the parameter keys that are passed. If you misspell a parameter (`attribute_type` vs. `type_attribute`), PyMISP will simply ignore it. This is dangerous insofar as you cannot determine on first glance that something went wrong, since PyMISP returns valid JSON.

My simple-minded approach would be to pop each parameter from `**kwargs` instead of getting them and alerting the user if arguments from the dict are not popped yet (`Unused parameter: attribute_type`). Perhaps you have a better idea.

issue_title: changes in dependencies of dependencies of charm-tools have broken usage on trusty venvs
issue_body:
On Aug 16, this worked:
```
virtualenv .venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
charm unavailable
charm-tools 2.1.3
```

As of Aug 17, it doesn't:
```
virtualenv .venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/venv/bin/charm-version", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
  File "/tmp/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 2720, in <module>
    parse_requirements(__requires__), Environment()
  File "/tmp/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 592, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist,req) # XXX put more info here
pkg_resources.VersionConflict: (setuptools 2.2 (/tmp/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages), Requirement.parse('setuptools>=11.3'))
```
This appears to be because secretstorage revved up on Aug 17:
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/SecretStorage

And the reqs lineage goes like so:
`cryptography->secretstorage->keyring->launchpadlib->charm-tools`

Setting an upper constraint for secretstorage seems to resolve the issue:
```
virtualenv venv
. .venv/bin/activate
pip install 'secretstorage<2.3.0'
pip install charm-tools
charm version
```
```
charm unavailable
charm-tools 2.1.3
```


I am using: Trusty

## Issue/Feature

### I expect/expected the following

Charm-tools should successfully install in a virtualenv on Trusty.


### What I got

Charm-tools has a dependency of a dependency that has changed, causing virtualenv charm-tools installations to break on Trusty.

issue_title: Using sas_clients queue when connecting to SAS Grid
issue_body:
Hello saspy team,

**Context:** 
We are using `saspy` and `sas_kernel` to connect to a SAS Grid. I was able to follow the setup instructions - thank you for making it so straight forward! - and now I can connect and submit code to the grid.

When I check the grid jobs, I noticed the LSF job started through saspy is running in the `normal` queue instead of the `sas_clients` queue. I think the wrinkle here is that the SASApp Workspace is setup to use the SASApp Grid Server to launch the LSF jobs. Currently there is NO default queue setup there and the queue the SAS clients get is based on an "option set". The option sets available are:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2080150/33023454-38f07646-cdd6-11e7-8915-bf92875c7e1b.png)


Since none of those are Jupyter, we are getting the default queue for the SASApp server context which is currently the "normal" queue. 

**Question:**
Is there any way to specify the `Grid Option Set` in the `sascfg.py` configuration to pass through to the IOM server and on down to LSF. If not it would seem that the only way to force that to happen would be to create an app server context for Jupyter and force it to have a default queue of sas_clients. I'm assuming that would work, but we would have to test and ideally would want to avoid that configuration overhead if possible.

Thanks!

issue_title: Data lost in attach/execute/logs due to raw socket handling code
issue_body: As discussed in #336 requests's socket reads may stray into the HTTP body, but the data will be kept in a local buffer for future reads. The HTTP response raw socket code bypasses this buffer to use the socket directly, which leads to the lost data.

issue_title: [1.7] regression in .dockerignore handling
issue_body:
If the `Dockerfile` is being ignored by a path in the `.dockerignore` file, it is incorrectly being removed from the context. There is a special case handling when the file is being excluded directly, but it should also apply when there is a path which includes the `Dockerfile`.

Possibly caused by #863

```
docker-py version: 1.7.0

$ tree -a
.
├── Docker
│   ├── dc.yml
│   └── Dockerfile.debug
└── .dockerignore

1 directory, 3 files

$ cat Docker/Dockerfile.debug

FROM alpine:3.3
RUN  echo building
CMD  echo run

$ cat Docker/dc.yml

app:
  build: ..
  dockerfile: Docker/Dockerfile.debug

$ cat .dockerignore
Docker/

docker-compose -f Docker/dc.yml build
ERROR: Cannot locate specified Dockerfile: Docker/Dockerfile.debug
```

issue_body:

Configuration file not found; the default location is: C:\Users\jp...\dwave\dwave.conf
Confirm configuration file path (editable):

try:

issue_title: Bug in VariableElimination
issue_body:
```python3
cpd_accident = TabularCPD('accident', 2, [[0.2], [0.8]])

cpd_traffic_jam = TabularCPD('traffic_jam', 2, [[0.9, 0.6, 0.7, 0.1],[0.1, 0.4, 0.3, 0.9]], evidence=['rain', 'accident'], evidence_card=[2, 2])

cpd_long_queues = TabularCPD('long_queues', 2, [[0.9, 0.2], [0.1, 0.8]], evidence=['traffic_jam'], evidence_card=[2])

cpd_getting_up_late = TabularCPD('getting_up_late', 2, [[0.6], [0.4]])

cpd_late_for_school = TabularCPD('late_for_school', 2, [[0.9, 0.45, 0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.55, 0.2, 0.9]], evidence=['getting_up_late', 'traffic_jam'], evidence_card=[2, 2])

cpd_rain = TabularCPD('rain', 2, [[0.4], [0.6]])

model = BayesianModel([('accident', 'traffic_jam'), ('rain', 'traffic_jam'), ('traffic_jam', 'late_for_school'), ('traffic_jam', 'long_queues'), ('getting_up_late', 'late_for_school')])

model.add_cpds(cpd_accident, cpd_traffic_jam, cpd_long_queues, cpd_getting_up_late, cpd_late_for_school, cpd_rain)

from pgmpy.inference import VariableElimination

infer = VariableElimination(model)

infer.query(['rain'], evidence={'traffic_jam': 1})

Traceback (most recent call last):

  File "<ipython-input-29-75d9f03ac72c>", line 1, in <module>
    infer.query(['rain'], evidence={'traffic_jam': 1})

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/inference/ExactInference.py", line 120, in query
    evidence=evidence, elimination_order=elimination_order)

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/inference/ExactInference.py", line 49, in _variable_elimination
    inplace=False)

  File "/home/ankur/pgmpy/pgmpy/factors/Factor.py", line 274, in reduce
    value_indices = list(map(int, value_row[1]))

ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'jam'
```
Happening because of our internal representation of variable with states (var_state) and since the variable name here is `traffic_jam`, it's splitting it from here.

https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/issues/392 should fix this also.
