issue_title: Bug in JointProbabilityDistribution.check_independence
issue_body:
In python 2 output is wrong while in python 3 output is correct .
Python 2:
```
In [1]: from pgmpy.factors import JointProbabilityDistribution as JPD

In [2]: import numpy as np

In [3]: prob = JPD(['x1','x2','x3'],[2,3,2], np.ones(12)/12)

In [4]: prob.check_independence('x1','x2')
Out[4]: False
```
Python 3
```
In [1]: from pgmpy.factors import JointProbabilityDistribution as JPD

In [2]: import numpy as np

In [3]: prob = JPD(['x1','x2','x3'],[2,3,2], np.ones(12)/12)

In [4]: prob.check_independence('x1','x2')
Out[4]: True
```
Couldn't quite figure out what caused this error. Only culprit could be marginalize method in Factors class, but it doesn't seem to do anything to cause such difference between the versions .
Weird thing is that get_independencies is returning the right output
```
In [1]: from pgmpy.factors import JointProbabilityDistribution as JPD

In [2]: import numpy as np

In [3]: prob = JPD(['x1','x2','x3'],[2,3,2], np.ones(12)/12)

In [4]: prob.check_independence('x1','x2')
Out[4]: False

In [5]: prob.get_independencies()
Out[5]: 
(x1 _|_ x2)
(x1 _|_ x3)
(x2 _|_ x3)
```
Ping @ankurankan Take a look at the code, and tell me what can be the cause of the bug .

```python
from pgmpy.models import MarkovModel
from pgmpy.factors import Factor
import numpy as np
mm = MarkovModel()
mm.add_edges_from([('a','a1'),('b','b1'),('a1','c1'),('b1','c1'),('a','b'),('a1','b1')])

mm.add_factors(*[factor for key,factor in factor_dict.items()])
jt = mm.to_junction_tree()
```

issue_title: Plans for 0.8.0 release
issue_body:
These are basic options that should go into 0.8.0:
- [x] Support the following question types in quizzes:
  - [x] reflect
  - [x] mcqReflect
- [x] Print course name along with an index: "Downloading python-for-everybody (1 / 13)"
- [x] Report list of courses where error during the schedule download has occurred
  - [x] Show full url to the course. It is useful to let the user know which courses require attention. User may visit such courses and enroll to them or switch sessions.
- [x] Ignore HTTP 403 errors when downloading syllabus (#568)
- [x] Add `--only-syllabus` option to download syllabus only and skip course contents (useful for developers)
- [x] Add `--download-delay` option that will make the script sleep for N seconds after downloading next course

These are "nice to have" options that not only may not go into 0.8.0, they may never be implemented, these are just my ideas:
- [ ] Understand course state better and provide suggestions, e.g. (#556):
  - You are not enrolled to the course. Enroll to the course and download again.
  - Your sessions has ended. Go to the course, switch sessions and download again.
  - Your course has not started yet. Please wait until it starts (25 September 2016, in X days) and download again.
  - Sample courses:
    - probabilistic-graphical-models
    - neural-networks
    - build-a-computer (OK)
- [ ] `--enroll` automatically enroll to the specified course
- [ ] `--switch-sessions` automatically switch sessions if the current one has expired
- [ ] `--summary` option to display:
  - [ ] failed URLs for each course
  - [ ] errors during syllabus download

issue_title: Introduce skip_<lines> topen arguments
issue_body:
# Overview

We could add the following `topen` arguments (it will be useful for all clients on top of tabulator like JTS or DP):
- skip_commented (with ability to set chars)
- skip_blank
- skip_initial (with ability to set count)

@danfowler said:

> There are CSVs that exist in the wild that begin with some number of commented lines: [example](https://portal.lternet.edu/nis/dataviewer?packageid=knb-lter-vcr.25.31&entityid=32a020479d23312012444a5b4ff81658) (45MB).  Default behavior of the library, of course, would be to assume there are no commented lines and read the data in as normal.  To support these kinds of files, we might want to have an `enable_comments` flag and optionally pass in a comment character (default to `#`).

issue_title: Be clearer about what .validate() does
issue_body:
It's not clear enough in the README that the `.validate()` method only validates that the descriptor is well formed according to the specification.  I think "validating a data package" might imply validating that the data is good too.  We should mention what this does and refer to Good Tables for full validation.  Probably also worth mentioning that retrieving the data will result in a `ValueError` if the data doesn't conform to the type specified in the schema.

https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-py#validating-a-data-package

issue_title: Pass `compression` argument to tabulator.Stream
issue_body:
As a dpp user, I want to be able to run pipelines on compressed (zip, gz) tabular data (csv, xsl(x)), so that I do not have to decompress them first and run pipelines after.

## Acceptance Criteria

* [ ] able to run pipelines with resource URLs that do not end with `.zip` but are valud compressed tabular data (See analysis)

## Tasks

* [ ] add `compression` to the list of params: https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines/blob/master/datapackage_pipelines/lib/stream_remote_resources.py#L136

## Analysis 

The above is completely possible if path or URL to my compressed file ends with correct extension Eg `.zip` as by default compression will be inferred from file name by the [tabulator](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tabulator-py#compression). but if url for my compressed file does not end with `.zip` pipelines will fail. 

For example, I have two identical zip files:
* zip file without extension https://datahub.io/zelima/zip-file-without-ext/r/zip-file.withoutext
* zip file with extension: https://datahub.io/zelima/zip-file-with-ext/r/zip-file.zip

If you create two identical `pipeline-spec.yaml` with a difference of only URLs from above and run them (Take a look this [gist](https://gist.github.com/zelima/afecc4f0428055cc6ec9a9f3ce7105ea#file-pipeline-spec-yaml)):
* one with extension runs fine
* one without extension fails. 

Above is happening cause `compression` is not on the list of parameters to pass to tabulator: https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines/blob/master/datapackage_pipelines/lib/stream_remote_resources.py#L136

issue_title: Slow task signature check and large task `~/.sos` folder
issue_body:
Also reported by @jean997 for a cluster specific feature. After doing lots of simulations on the cluster the external task signature managed by `sos task` accumulates to the point that it appears to be hanging when submitting new tasks. 

As discussed on slack, ways to resolve the issue is:

1. Automatically clean up old tasks (but how often? after every run is overkill)
2. Use time stamps instead just like snakemake (but will have problems of skewed time stamp -- time stamps is not reliable across machines)

So compromised alternatives are:

1. Clean up tasks at intervals of days.
2. Use time stamps for failed and completed tasks only. 

I think this should solve most of the problem though there are still room to optimize. Will apply these changes later and see what happens. If it is still to conservative and performance still is an issue, we can certainly explore more aggressive options.

issue_title: proposal: use a platform-agnostic database migration tool
issue_body:
We would like to use a tool other than Django South or Django 1.7 migrations to manage the controller's schema changes and data migrations. As we see in #1756, moving between South in Django 1.6 and migrations in 1.7 is not painless. And using a neutral framework like [goose](https://bitbucket.org/liamstask/goose) (written in go, but we would integrate it as a command-line tool) makes the idea of someday rewriting the controller itself in go more plausible.

 * Migrations still need run idempotently on each controller boot
 * We need to handle data migrations as well as schema migrations
 * Must be 100% backwards compatible with South

issue_title: sync system clock before starting etcd/fleet
issue_body:
In our cloud-config, we sync the system clock using NTP: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/dbd42a7352759f9ac98cbd59bf20ac757672bc6d/contrib/coreos/user-data.example#L46-L58

The cloudinit service parses and starts these services in-order, meaning that currently we're changing the system time after etcd has been started. I'm wondering if this could potentially result in etcd getting out of sync between nodes. We should move this service to start before etcd and fleet.

issue_title: Proposal: Deis Connector - A way to connect between two Deis
issue_body:
We were thinking about a secure and reliable way for containers running in separated Deis installations (and probably in production) to comunicate with each other.
The idea begins because we have two Deis installation on each environment. We have a Deis "Web Facing" and a Deis "Private".
The Web Facing Deis only receive and send https connections and the Private Deis only receive https connections but he can send tcp/http connections to resources / services like MySQL, Redis, MongoDB and more.

The initial idea was:

{ **WEB** }  *---(https)->* [ **Deis Web Facing** ] *---(https)->* [ **Deis Private** ]  *---(tcp/http)->* **Resource**

But we think that we can modify this connection between the two Deis installations and use a "connector" based on the AWS IAM way, using a KEY/SECRET and a JSON allowing / denying the access. 

##### Example of Deis Connector:
 - Frontend (Web Facing)
 - API (Private)
 - MySQL (Recurso)
 - DeisConnectorKey:  ``80MNOUQADpSu``
 - DeisConnectorPass: ``4QWU8f4ei1pnKEfyPN9Sr36X``
 - DeisConnectorJSON Example:

```json
{
	"DeisAuthorizations": [
		{
			"Action": "Access",
			"Effect": "Allow",
			"Rules": [
				{
					"Origin": "web-app.paas.comp.org",
					"Destination": "api-app.paas.com.org",
					"ConnectionType": "http"
				}
			]
		}
	]
}
```

With this, we can control between a module/app of own Deis the communication of external containers that belong to the same "Stack". Its very helpful in a environment like production where we want to limit the access.

issue_title: Add `autopep8` diff to linting validation?
issue_body:
@jimPruyne recently submitted a PR with what I considered a noisy diff, but it turns out that diff was generated by `autopep8` in his workflow.

We can easily see what would change with an `autopep8` run on the whole repo with `autopep8 -r --diff globus_sdk`
It doesn't look like much to me, and none of the things it changes are things I feel very strongly about (so long as we're consistent, really).

`autopep8` is a good tool and I want to be friendly towards its usage.
Should we do a single run of `autopep8 -r --in-place`, and then add the diff command to Travis?
Or do a single run of it `--in-place` but not enforce it in Travis (which is more accepting, but could lead to noise when someone does run autopep8 on a module)?

@jaswilli, @corpulentcoffee, your feedback would be appreciated.

issue_title: Make testsuite concurrency-safe
issue_body:
This is a known problem discussed partly in #120 
Many thanks to @aaschaer for his work on the testsuite (!), but he noted that there are issues with concurrency safety.

We should rectify this.

A couple of things we can do to start:
- [ ] Normal teardown should only delete assets created during *this* test run
- [ ] Tests should track all assets that are created and delete them all on teardown
- [ ] Complete cleanup (e.g. delete all endpoints belonging to user) should be a manual task runnable from the repo, just like generating a new refresh token
- [ ] Tests which use shared resources (e.x. Tutorial Endpoints) should insert random, unique strings into any identifiers used

This will get us into a better state, and we can attack any remaining issues as they arise.

issue_title: Meta: Overhaul label system
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The way we currently use labels could be improved to enable better issue discovery.

### How can it be fixed

I'd like to overhaul and unify the labels in this repo and potentially apply the same rules to other repos as well. This is inspired by https://github.com/machinelabs/machinelabs which in turn is inspired by https://github.com/angular/angular

Here are two secreenshots to give an idea what it could look like.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/521109/42214872-ff6b4098-7ebc-11e8-9101-fd20fc710f82.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/521109/42214885-0770b4d0-7ebd-11e8-82bc-760d02d9a545.png)

The gist of it goes like this.

We would create labels that fall into the following categories:

`comp: <component-name>` (e.g. `comp: p2p`, `comp: trinity`, `comp: benchmark`)
`type: <type>` (e.g. `type: feature`, `type: bug`, `type: docs`)
`effort: <effort-estimate>` (e.g. `effort: hours`, `effort: days`, `effort: weeks`)
`prio: <prio>` (e.g. `prio: P0-critical`, `prio: P1-urgent`, `prio: P2-required`)
`PR-state: <state>` (e.g. `PR-state: wip`, `PR-state: needs review`, `PR-state: blocked`)

All the labels that fall into the same category share the same color (as seen on the screenshot). Nothing set in stone regarding concrete labels but I like the core idea of it. I'm happy to do the migration work we want to go that way.

issue_labels: [{'id': 631592722, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw2MzE1OTI3MjI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/Good%20First%20Issue', 'name': 'Good First Issue', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 815420353, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MTU0MjAzNTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20trinity', 'name': 'comp: trinity', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 983629570, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2Mjk1NzA=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/effort:%20E1%20hours', 'name': 'effort: E1 hours', 'color': 'bfe5bf', 'default': False}, {'id': 983614747, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTQ3NDc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20feature', 'name': 'type: feature', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}, {'id': 983615043, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTUwNDM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20performance', 'name': 'type: performance', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

It's expected that we hit this method quite often:

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/46f956ea048eafed6606f59a465af375f1be7a9a/trinity/tx_pool/validators.py#L47-L56

As innocent as it seems on first glance, this is hitting the `ChainProxy` which means there's inter-process communication involved, making this a good target for some caching.

### How can it be fixed

User a time based caching. Max life time of cached entry should be 5 minutes. Maybe we could use a convenience decorator such as [`@cachetools.func.ttl_cache`](http://cachetools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#cachetools.func.ttl_cache).

issue_title: Do not use hard-coded peers for ChainSyncer/StateDownloader
issue_body:
Those two services use the ETH sub-protocol, which is supported by most nodes in the network (unlike LES), so instead of using a `PeerPool` with hard-coded nodes[1] they should use one that employs `p2p.DiscoveryProtocol` to find and connect to as many peers as possible.

[1] That is a dirty hack we needed because the discovery v4 protocol is terrible at finding LES nodes (#429)

issue_title: Help of miniconda windows installer is missing doc for silent install mode
issue_body:
I was using the Miniconda windows installer from http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html --- and happily found out that a silent install mode is supported. It took quite a while to discover that, as the doc shown by running the installer with the `/?` option does not show these options at all. Running  `Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /?` will show a window with:
```
Usage Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe [options]
Options:

/InstallationType=AllUsers [default: JustMe]

/AddToPath=[0|1] [default: 1]

/RegisterPython=[0|1] [default: AllUsers: 1, JustMe: 0]

Examples:
Install for all users, but don't add to PATH env var:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /InstallationType=AllUsers
/AddToPath=0

Install for just me, but register the system Python:
Miniconda-3.7.0-Windows-x86.exe /RegisterPython=1
```

This option list is missing the available options
```
/S

/D=C:\Miniconda
```
for silent install mode (unattended install mode) and the installation directory respectively.

Also the documentation on http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html, or http://conda.pydata.org/docs/index.html are not mentioning the command line options of the installer for conda / miniconda.

The only hint I could find was: http://docs.continuum.io/anaconda/install.html#windows-install, which itself is not mentioning the supported options  `AddToPath` `RegisterPython` `InstallationType`.

issue_title: source activate is broken for zsh 4.3.10
issue_body:
I am using Anaconda on an older Linux box with zsh 4.3.10.  As of 9166defcbdc892aa7c466b9511fe74f36e0c7d5a the `source activate` exits my shell:

```zsh
$ source /path/to/anaconda/bin/activate
Error: activate must be sourced. Run 'source activate envname'
instead of 'activate envname'.
# shell exits
```
This happens because zsh 4.3.10 does not have the `ZSH_EVAL_CONTEXT` parameter yet and the [_conda_script_is_sourced](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/9d759d8edeb86569c25f6eb82053f09581013a2a/conda/shell/bin/activate#L39-L53) then returns an incorrect result.

@kalefranz - can you please fix that function so it works with zsh 4.3.10?

If it is critical to enforce sourcing of the `activate` script, I feel it would be much simpler to just remove its executable permission.  It would be then impossible to run it otherwise than by sourcing and you could go away with all the complicated tests in `_conda_script_is_sourced`.

On 4.6 on canary
```bash

```
It fails.

Removing an option from the CLI in a minor version sounds like a no no. Could we instead deprecate (even if the option does not do anything?)

issue_title: Make conda's docs versioned
issue_body:
Conda doesn't use ReatTheDoc which is fine - I assume there are good reasons for it, however the conda docs seem to be missing some features as compared to RTD.

Specifically the docs don't seem to be tied to any particular version of `conda`. I assume that [https://conda.io/docs/](https://conda.io/docs/) always just points to the latest stable release, but sometimes you want to read up on new features in unreleased versions, or to actually find the docs for the specific version you're currently running which may not always be the latest stable.

RTD allows you to view the docs for any past version and for the upcoming, unreleased version which is a *very* useful feature. It would be good if the conda docs allowed you to do the same. The most important aspect for me is to be able to (easily) read up about the new features which have been developed but not yet released.

Is that actually correct that those docs aren't published or have I just missed them somewhere?

issue_title: Move _version.py into your namespace
issue_body: Currently `_version.py` is installed into `/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/_version.py` or `/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/_version.py`. There is no indication that `_version.py` belongs to configobj and could conflict with other project that do the same. So please rename _version.py to `configobj_version.py`, move it into a subdirectory, or completely get rid of it.

issue_title: UTF-8 serialization in python 2
issue_body:
I'm running python 2.7 connecting to AWS elasticsearch service using the 2.2 release of elasticsearch-py.  To connect I use requests_aws4auth as recommended in your docs (thanks for integrating that!).

When writing to elasticsearch (bulk upload, creating a doc etc) I get this error:

```UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 2102: ordinal not in range(128)```

I know that the library was changed a couple months ago to stop hiding unicode errors but this change coincides with the introduction of support for requests_aws4auth as they are both first seen in the 2.2 release and therefore downgrading is not an option for me.  Handling unicode conversion myself piecemeal is non-trivial.  Upgrading to python 3 is not an option yet given other dependencies.

Therefore, I have come up with a workaround for now using a custom serializer that essentially reverts the unicode change made earlier to this codebase:

```
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch, RequestsHttpConnection, serializer, compat, exceptions

class JSONSerializerPython2(serializer.JSONSerializer):
    """Override elasticsearch library serializer to ensure it encodes utf characters during json dump.
    See original at: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/serializer.py#L42
    A description of how ensure_ascii encodes unicode characters to ensure they can be sent across the wire
    as ascii can be found here: https://docs.python.org/2/library/json.html#basic-usage
    """
    def dumps(self, data):
        # don't serialize strings
        if isinstance(data, compat.string_types):
            return data
        try:
            return json.dumps(data, default=self.default, ensure_ascii=True)
        except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
            raise exceptions.SerializationError(data, e)
```

I hope this helps anyone else that runs into this issue.

issue_title: resource show: data loss during SDK deserialization
issue_body:
//cc: @williexu, @tjprescott, @lmazuel 

For services which have the response body not conforming to recommended format by ARM, we will lose data when Python SDK tries to de-serialize to GenericResource. I understand the right, long-term way is to have service teams fix it, but at the same time I suggest expose a flag like `--include-response-body` to include the json response-body in the result. This will help customers migrate to CLI 2.0 soon using generic resource commands

Repro:
```bash
az resource show --id /subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/resourceGroups/yuganwgase/providers/Microsoft.Web/hostingEnvironments/yugangwase-1/capacities/virtualip --api-version 2016-09-01

issue_body:
Current command for creating elastic-pool requires `parameters` of type `azure.mgmt.sql.models.ElasticPool` to be supplied over command line.

Example:
```
(env) vishrut@visshamac azure-cli (sql-db-test) $ az sql elastic-pools create -h

Command
    az sql elastic-pools create: Creates a new Azure SQL elastic pool or updates an existing Azure
    SQL elastic pool.

Arguments
    --elastic-pool-name [Required]: The name of the Azure SQL Elastic Pool to be operated on
                                    (Updated or created).
    --parameters        [Required]: The required parameters for creating or updating an Elastic
                                    Pool.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group.
    --server-name       [Required]: The name of the Azure SQL server.

Global Arguments
    --debug                       : Increase logging verbosity to show all debug logs.
    --help -h                     : Show this help message and exit.
    --output -o                   : Output format.  Allowed values: json, jsonc, list, table, tsv.
                                    Default: json.
    --query                       : JMESPath query string. See http://jmespath.org/ for more
                                    information and examples.
    --verbose                     : Increase logging verbosity. Use --debug for full debug logs.
``` 

We should expand `ElasticPool` model instead of having supply `parameters`. 

Minor PR for the fix will follow shortly. :)

Thanks

issue_title: Storage authentication parameters are cumbersome to use
issue_body:
Storage commands such as az container list require the connection string, SaS, account name, etc.  These make sense as optional parameters but shouldn't be required, since you're already authenticated and can retrieve the connection string, SaS, etc via other commands.

Presently, you have to call two commands in the common case--one to get the SaS and one to query the container.  There should be one command.  

The support for environment variables and global config isn't helpful in situations where you're interacting with more than one storage account, which is also quite common.

issue_title: `client_factory` kwarg bug in KnackConversion branch
issue_body:
When using the `custom_command()` method to register a command, the client_factory being used is taken from the `command_type` kwarg.

I tried to load the [data/kuka_iiwa](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/tree/master/data/kuka_iiwa) models as detailed in this [v-rep forum post](http://www.forum.coppeliarobotics.com/viewtopic.php?f=5&t=6712) and in the latest gazebo 8.1:

Warning [parser.cc:781] XML Element[contact], child of element[link] not defined in SDF. Ignoring[contact]. You may have an incorrect SDF file, or an sdformat version that doesn't support this element.
Warning [parser.cc:781] XML Element[contact], child of element[link] not defined in SDF. Ignoring[contact]. You may have an incorrect SDF file, or an sdformat version that doesn't support this element.

I also tried kuka_world.sdf in gazebo 8.1:

○ /usr/local/Cellar/gazebo8/8.1.0/bin/gazebo /Users/athundt/source/bullet3/data/kuka_iiwa/kuka_world.sdf
2017-05-28 17:52:16.111 gzclient[35216:565505] Layout still needs update after calling -[QNSPanelContentsWrapper layout].  QNSPanelContentsWrapper or one of its superclasses may have overridden -layout without calling super. Or, something may have dirtied layout in the middle of updating it. issue_body:

Intermittently, the server sends a frame that is split in multiple TCP/IP packets which seems to be the problem for the websocket client implementation in aiohttp. I observe the errors like : "Received frame with non-zero reserved bits" or no response to PING which drops the connection as 'Idle'.

The example is PING which is normally send in one single packet(packet 10902) and parsed correctly by the library but when it comes split in two packets(11135, 11136), it's not recognized as PING. I put the debug line in http_websocket.py to see what's coming in to parse_frame()


http_websocket.py

issue_title: Add custom header support to websocket client
issue_body:
## Feature

Websocket protocol actually supports custom headers. They are not widely used (afair you cannot access them in webbrowser JS in any reasonable way), but may be important.

I've encountered this issue while developing a WS proxy on aiohttp - proxy naturally wants to achieve best 
content 'forwarding' possible.

Current state in aiohttp:

* Server side websocket handler can set handlers;
* Client side `ws_connect` can set headers, but it is not documented;
* Websocket response (`ClientWebSocketResponse` class) doesn't allow to access them.

So, it looks like there are 2 things to do:

* Add `.headers` property to ClientWebSocketResponse
* Document `headers` kwarg to `ClientSession.ws_connect()`

issue_title: True read-only cells
issue_body:
nbgrader currently has pseudo "read-only" cells where they are "read-only" in the sense that their contents get saved to the nbgrader database, and then overwritten during grading if they have been changed by the student. I have reports that this is confusing, and requests for true read-only cells where the cell actually cannot be edited at all in the live notebook.

I think this would be really useful to have for nbgrader. We could keep it as an undocumented feature for the moment, just like "undeletable" cells are, until we can figure out an appropriate UI for it.

issue_title: Only typeset displayed data if needed
issue_body:
In https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/39a706c9cfc1be96779559c02c95474b03afbd6a/notebook/static/notebook/js/outputarea.js#L651, the entire output area is typeset if any display_data message has an html, markdown or latex mimetype. This is problematic:

1. Just because the mimetype exists doesn't mean it was the one that was rendered. We should be checking to see if the latex, html, or markdown mimetype was actually the one displayed.
2. We shouldn't typeset the entire output area, just the display data that we just added.

This came up in  https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/1777. #2444 may also be related.

issue_title: Run Notebook server for multiple users
issue_body:
Disclaimer: This is not really an issue, but more of a question and I did not know where else to ask this (a mailing list?).

I want to deploy one (or more than one) notebook server online, so that many people can access it without login and they all have their own notebook instances of a single notebook file, so that no user sees any modifications of another user or can use their Python kernels with all the variables set. I do not need any login. In fact any login would increase the obstacles to use my notebook and is undesirable in my scenario. I don't want people having to sign up for anything. They shall remain anonymous. I want this for allowing anyone to test my notebook.

I know about the project JupyterHub, however, I'd like to avoid using it for such open structure. From working with JupyterHub before, I know that JupyterHub is a huge amount of unstructured code (thousands of lines in one file, every object talking to every other object, no UML diagrams in documentation to help getting an overview, hard to refactor anything without breaking stuff). It is functioning, but it does much more than I need and on a first search, I could not even find the docs for disabling user logins in it. If I have no choice, I'll use it, but it requires me to dabble in so many different things like user database, which spawner to use, how to configure the spawner, how to disable login completely, resource management per configuration files and probably others.
I'd rather take one or two days and write my own minimal server, on top of some of the minimalistic webservers Python has, which simply uses dockerpy to spawn a container for each visitor of some route on my server with some predefined resource limitation. I'd have to think about how to link an anonymous visitor with the respective container though.

Is there a way to use plain notebook servers (for example inside a docker container) in such an open way for multiple users, without one user being able to see context of another user?
Or maybe any minimal multi user server, which is lightweight in comparison to JupyterHub?
Is writing my own realistic, or is linking visitors to their respective docker containers a difficult task?

issue_title: feature request: comment out markdown blocks but still export for 'download as...' option
issue_body:
Is there a way to have this feature for the 'download as...' 

For example.

Spin up an R notebook via

[tmpnb.org
](tmpnb.org)

```file > download as > R (.r)```

the resulting .R file only keeps the R code while stripping out the content of the original jupyter notebook markdown block.

The use case for this is when people  

*1) first prototype in jupyter notebook and keep medium length notes in a jupyter notebook markdown block
*2) then migrate to a more powerful IDE to build on the previous prototype

If you try that workflow now, the useful jupyter notebook markdown content is lost when you switch to 2.

issue_title: Recommended method of installation
issue_body:
The jupyter website recommends to install anaconda in order to get jupyter up and running.

I did a clean install of anaconda (for python 3.4) with the latest sh script of their website, added the bin folder to my Path and executed:

    conda update conda
    conda update anaconda
    conda update python
    conda install jupyter
    conda update jupyter

now jupyter is available in my shell, I can start both notebooks in the browser and as qtconsoles.

But there is a problem: It is impossible to import pyplot from matplotlib. Here is the pastebin http://pastebin.com/RJ0Mj2F0

I tried to install pygobject with pip, but apparently anaconda has its own copy of the python interpreter. Pygobject or pygtk are not in the anaconda channels.

This command:
    anaconda search -t conda pygtk

issue_title: New users going to text editor, confused about what to do
issue_body:
See ipython/ipython#10291, but this is also an issue I've seen working with people in person. People have heard that Jupyter is the thing to use, so they install it, open up a `.py` file, and then wonder: How do I run the code? Where are all the buttons I see in screenshots of Jupyter? What's so great about having a text editor in my web browser?

Can we better direct people towards notebooks? Could we find a way that the first thing people see in Jupyter is a notebook interface rather than a file list?

One obvious response is to improve the text editor and better integrate it with our kernel machinery. I think I've seen that this is what Jupyterlab does. But there are other projects expending masses of effort on making text editors and IDEs, and I don't think we're going to make something world-class in that space. Jupyter's central value is notebooks, so even with a better text editor, I think it's worth thinking about how we make it obvious to new users to create a notebook.

issue_title: base/js/dialog.js implicitly requires bootstrap, should be made explicit
issue_body:
The modal.modal(...) call on [line 126](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/base/js/dialog.js#L126) implicitly requires bootsrap to have been loaded for the modal call to succeed.
This appears not to be a problem for most of the notebook, since bootstrap will already be loaded, but for any extensions, it would be nice to make the dependency explicit by, e.g., calling require('bootstrap') in a similar manner to that used in [lines 7-8](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/base/js/dialog.js#L7-L8).

issue_title: Model + Schema
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am working on making latest Schematics work with SQLAlchemy (see https://github.com/schematics/schemalchemy/issues/1) by bringing calculated types (getter/setter functions) up-to-date.

The core issue is to have the model instance go through the import loop, so that custom user setters can run against the instance.

Towards this goal, I ended up splitting the Model into a simpler structure called Schema, similar to what SQLAlchemy does with its Table vs Base types.

The current work is ongoing in my repo at:
https://github.com/nKey/schematics/tree/calculated-type

What does this mean in practice? The Model now wraps the data and encodes the usual lib workflow, while the Schema encodes the actual type structure. This means any field name can fit into the Schema (no Model method conflict), and people that need more complex usage can rely on the backend without needing a Model.

Example:

``` python
    schema = Schema('Player',
        Field('id', IntType()),
        Field('first_name', StringType(required=True)),
        Field('last_name', StringType(required=True)),
        Field('full_name', calculated(
            type=StringType(),
            fget=get_full_name,
            fset=set_full_name))
    )
    input_data = {'id': '1', 'full_name': 'John Doe', 'hi': 1}
    converted_data = convert(schema, input_data, partial=True)
    validated_data = validate(schema, converted_data, convert=False, partial=True)
```

This issue is just a notice I am working on this, it is not ready for a PR and it breaks the API (although I am making compatibility mixins).

PS: any and all feedback is welcome

issue_title: How it work ? if you can make a simple example
Problem with vector assignment
issue_body:
I have encountered a problem when using autograd on specific functions that I am not able to solve. It occurs when a function contains a local variable that is a numpy array in which intermediate results are stored. I can probably best explain this by means of the following minimum example:

```python
from autograd import grad
from autograd import numpy as np

eta = np.random.standard_normal((5, 3))

# autograd works
def f1(param):
    res = np.sum(param * param[0], axis=1)
    return np.sum(res, axis=0)
print('f1:', f1(eta))
ag_f1 = grad(f1, 0)
print(ag_f1(eta))


# autograd does not work
def f2(param):
    res = np.zeros(5)
    for m in range(5):
        res[m] = np.sum(param[m] * param[0])
    return np.sum(res)
print('f2:', f2(eta))
ag_f2 = grad(f2, 0)
print(ag_f2(eta))

```

These two functions are equivalent, and should result in the same output and gradient.

This results in the following output:

```python
bruno$ python scratch.py
f1: 0.640560618546
[[-0.48734979  1.77265289  1.7501133 ]
 [-0.64951507  1.3475087   0.1051734 ]
 [-0.64951507  1.3475087   0.1051734 ]
 [-0.64951507  1.3475087   0.1051734 ]
 [-0.64951507  1.3475087   0.1051734 ]]
f2: 0.640560618546
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/core.py", line 26, in forward_pass
    try: end_node = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py", line 19, in scalar_fun
    return as_scalar(fun(*args, **kwargs))
  File "scratch.py", line 19, in f2
    res[m] = np.sum(param[m] * param[0])
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "scratch.py", line 23, in <module>
    print(ag_f2(eta))
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py", line 25, in gradfun
    vjp, ans = make_vjp(scalar_fun, argnum)(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/core.py", line 14, in vjp
    start_node, end_node = forward_pass(fun, args, kwargs, argnum)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/core.py", line 27, in forward_pass
    except Exception as e: add_extra_error_message(e)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/errors.py", line 57, in add_extra_error_message
    raise_(etype, value, traceback)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/future/utils/__init__.py", line 414, in raise_
    raise exc.with_traceback(tb)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/core.py", line 26, in forward_pass
    try: end_node = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bruno/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py", line 19, in scalar_fun
    return as_scalar(fun(*args, **kwargs))
  File "scratch.py", line 19, in f2
    res[m] = np.sum(param[m] * param[0])
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```

So the functions `f1` and `f2` return the same output, but I am not able to take the gradient of `f2` using autograd. The above is of course a very stylized example, but in some more complex code I am running in essentially the same problem. Is there a way to solve this error? And am I missing something? Based on the restrictions in the autograd tutorial these operations should be OK as far as I can tell.

Pip 10.0.0b1 was released, with support for `pyproject.toml`. This allows a project to specify that it needs `scikit-build` in the setup phase, allowing for the first time a simple "pip install package" for a scikit-build package. `pyproject.tomls` should be added to the examples in tests and documtentation.

On a related note, `cmake` could be added as a dependency as well, but since a user may have cmake, that's probably better in `setup_requires` or maybe not at all. If scikit-build could gain a new `require_cmake_version` argument, which would then check for the cmake command and ask it's version, then add the python 'cmake' as a requirement if this does not pass, that might be ideal.

issue_title: incorrect `total_rdy` update logic when sending new `RDY`count
issue_body:
In `Reader._send_rdy()` the following block is responsible for updating the reader's `total_rdy` attribute:
```
if conn.send_rdy(value):
      self.total_rdy = max(self.total_rdy - conn.rdy + value, 0)
```
Here `value` represents the desired new `RDY` count; thus if `conn.send_rdy(value)` succeeds we'll have `conn.rdy == value`.  As a result the above will leave `total_rdy` unchanged.   Since there's no other spot in which `total_rdy` is increased, it never gets above its initial value of 0. 

One behavioral consequence of this:  Even if `max_in_flight` is less than the `nsqd` connection count, every connection will get an initial `RDY` count of 1, and so the `max_in_flight` constraint can be violated immediately after a reader begins consuming messages.

For comparison, the go client does this:
```
atomic.AddInt64(&r.totalRdyCount, -c.RDY()+count)
err := c.WriteCommand(Ready(int(count)))
```

issue_title: Document how to increase the plugin log level to debug
issue_body:
This is the synapse config I used to increase the log level for the LDAP plugin:

```
version: 1

formatters:
  precise:
   format: '%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(lineno)d - %(levelname)s - %(request)s- %(message)s'

filters:
  context:
    (): synapse.util.logcontext.LoggingContextFilter
    request: ""

handlers:
  file:
    class: logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler
    formatter: precise
    filename: /path/to/synapse/homeserver.log
    maxBytes: 104857600
    backupCount: 10
    filters: [context]
    level: DEBUG
  console:
    class: logging.StreamHandler
    formatter: precise
    filters: [context]

loggers:
    synapse:
        level: INFO

    synapse.storage.SQL:
        level: INFO

    ldap3:
        level: DEBUG

    ldap_auth_provider:
        level: DEBUG

root:
    level: INFO
    handlers: [file, console]
```

List of changes from the default generated config:
* Set `handlers -> file -> level` to `DEBUG`
* Added the `loggers -> ldap3` and `loggers -> ldap_auth_provider` sections, both with `level: DEBUG`

This should be documented in the repository somewhere, perhaps in the main README.

issue_title: charms.reactive.relations assumes the relation name is a key
issue_body:
The RelationBase and Conversation model assumes that a relation name name is a key, rather than using the relation id + unit name. This will cause a reactive charm to fail, depending how the related service chooses to use it. For example, here is a metadata.yaml snippet from a client that stores its data in three separate silos:

```yaml
requires:
    session:
        interface: pgsql
    public:
        interface: pgsql
    confidential:
        interface: pgsql
```

On the client side, everything is fine. However, on the server side we have this:

```yaml
provides:
    db:
        interface: pgsql
```

If the client is related three times to the same server, which would be common for development and staging, then the reactive model fails as the relation name can not be used as a key to identify which of the three db interfaces is needed. The relation id must be used.

issue_title: Make `read`'s `ignore` argument easier to manipulate
issue_body:
The `read` method has this signature:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=()):
```

The `ignore` argument is a tuple of field names that NailGun should not try to populate when reading data from the server. For example, this is the signature of `DockerComputeResource.read`:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=('password',)):
```

This method signature works well if one wants to ignore a certain method attribute at all times. However, this method signature does not work well if one wants to conditionally ignore some field due to a bug. For example, this bit of code is used in response to a specific bug:

```python
ignore = set(ignore).union([…])  # list comprehension
if 'errata_id' in attrs:
    ignore.discard('errata')  # pylint:disable=no-member
ignore = tuple(ignore)
```

In the above code snippet, `ignore` is converted to a set, manipulated, then converted back to a tuple. Unwieldy idioms like this are likely to become more prevalent as more field-specific bugs are discovered - especially when those bugs are fixed in certain Satellite versions.

An empty tuple is used as the default argument to `read` because:

* tuples are immutable
* the field should default to an empty iterable

However, as described above, tuples are hard to work with. Some better solution should be conceived and implemented.

One alternative it to make `read` have this signature:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=None):
```

And somewhere in the call stack, `ignore` would be given a default value:

```python
if ignore is None:
    ignore = set()
```

This would work decently well in cases where an argument should always be ignored:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=None):
    if ignore is None:
        ignore = set()
    ignore.add('password')
```

It would also work very well in cases where more interesting manipulations need to happen:

```python
ignore.add([…])  # list comprehension
if 'errata_id' in attrs:
    ignore.discard('errata')  # pylint:disable=no-member
```

This solution would retain the two advantages listed earlier:

* <del>tuples</del><ins>None</ins> is immutable
* the field should default to an empty iterable

issue_title: Reduce time for serverless cold-start time of .Net/C# code
issue_body:
Reduce time for serverless cold-start time of .Net/C# code

At the moment .Net Core is one of the slowest loading languages you can use for serverless development in Azure Functions of Aws Lambda. Each time you start a new function written in .Net Core there is  significant "cold-start" time which means users experience an extended time waiting compared to using other languages. With serverless becoming a core architectural approach for many companies it would make sense to look into this problem now. If the "cold start" of .net core programs could be reduced to low milliseconds it would significantly increase the appeal of the language for serverless development.

The second part of this problem is the artifact that is generated from a dotnet core publish is large compared to other languages which also attributes to slow "cold-start" times. It would be good if the IL Linker could be brought into the normal publishing process to reduce dll sizes (https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions.md). If namespaces or classes are not being used they should be removed from the published dlls. Or combine dlls into a single binary that only contains used classes/methods/properties. Regardless, just make it super small and fast to work the best out of all languages with serverless.

issue_title: Allow for enrolling certs for renewal without installer integration?
issue_body:
Right now my understanding is that we never create a RenewableCert lineage with a renewal config and associated files within `/etc/letsencrypt` unless we installed the cert into a server with configuration changes via an installer plugin.

Some discussions on the forum seem to suggest that this may be too restrictive because people might want renewable lineages to exist and be available for use with their own server configurations (like servers that we don't officially support via installers). This makes sense and I think @jdkasten and I had considered this possibility before.

Is this right? How should it work? How should users choose this behavior as opposed to just saving the PEM files in their working directory?

issue_title: remove python-ndg-httpsclient dependency on modern Python platforms
issue_body: If the available python platform supports secure TLS connections natively, we don't need to depend on `python-ndg-httpsclient`.

issue_title: Augeas 1.4 is still broken (doesn't like mod_rewrite and regular expressions)
issue_body:
I've identified another issue with augeas.

I already know about #981 and I was able to patch my own system to avoid that issue; However, it seems that even the new httpd lens fails when a config file includes regular expressions.

I have 11 config files in my <sites-enabled> folder, and augeas reports “syntax errors” for exactly those files which make use of mod_rewrite. mod_rewrite is an apache module which uses regular expressions for configuration.

At this point, I would suggest that we patch the lens ourselves as we wouldn't want to wait for a new version of augeas.

issue_title: renewal notice : email should sequence the domain names correctly
issue_body:
#### Background

Suppose a certificate was created using a command of the form
> letsencrypt-auto run -d www.example.com -d example.com ....

Note the order in which the domain names appear.

#### Observed behavior

The renewal email mentions the relevant domain names in some wacky order:
> example.com, www.example.com

#### Desired behavior

* It should mention them in the correct order ... at least with the correct name first.  This is important, because the certificate files are named after the **first** domain name mentioned on the command line, as discussed in item #1376.

* The email should be even more explicit:  It should say like:
> To renew the same set of domain names, pass the following options on the command line:
> -d www.example.com -d example.com

issue_title: Relative links
issue_body: https://github.com/letsencrypt/acme-spec/issues/128; if relative links are not allowed, we should at least log an appropriate error message and notify the user, or be clever and try to normalize them based on `Location` or request URI if the former is not present.

issue_title: letsencrypt renew should imply non-interactive
issue_body: I think this behavior is somewhat confusing, but multiple other contributors want this change and we're moving forward with it. It simplifies running `letsencrypt renew` in cron.

issue_title: Engagment Tracking always get set on transmission
issue_body: Engagement tracking flags always get set on transmissions no matter if they are sent or not. This prevents the ability to fall back to the settings set on a saved template for these values.

issue_title: RFC822 timestamp using GMT is parsed into BST if local time is UK time
issue_body:
The following code snipped prints `True` unless the machine is using UK time.

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.parser import parse
from dateutil.tz import tzutc


d1 = datetime(2002, 10, 2, 13, 0, tzinfo=tzutc())
d2 = parse('Wed, 02 Oct 2002 13:00:00 GMT')
print(d1 == d2)
```

It appears that at [this point](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser.py#L605) in the parse function, the parser sees that local time is one of `('GMT', 'BST')` because in the Summer the UK uses BST (GMT+1).

Source issue: boto/botocore#1070

issue_body:

issue_title: ElasticSearch.create_index sends PUT request with body=None
issue_body:
The `send_request` helper defaults `body` to `''`, but `create_index` defaults `body` to `settings`, which in turn, defaults to `None`, so `send_request` ends up with `body=None`. This causes problems when going down to `httplib` via `urllib3` because `httplib` doesn't set a `Content-Length` header for any requests where the `body` is `None`. All of this amounts to 411 responses from nginx when using that as a proxy for ElasticSearch.

I've actually submitted a bug report for Python[1] because I think that even with `body=None`, `httplib` should set the `Content-Length` header for methods that expect a body (`PATCH`, `POST`, and `PUT`) but in the meantime, we could avoid the issue either by not passing `body` to `send_request` from `create_index` if it's `None`, or (even better) have some logic inside `send_request` that defaults `body` to `None` and sets it to `''` if that's how it comes in.

[1] http://bugs.python.org/issue23539

issue_title: New feature for iterating over fields_dict in marshalling/unmarshalling
issue_body:
jsonschema(and swagger) have specific options for unexpected additional fields.
- [additionalProperties](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#model-with-mapdictionary-properties)
- [patternProperties(maybe, jsonschema only)](http://json-schema.org/example2.html#base-schema)

And, I want to translate this feature to marshmallow schema.

## marshmallow's current implementation

The way of iteration in marshmallow's current implementation, is just calling `iteritems()` with fields_dict. So, it is difficult that define schema allowing unexpected field.

for examples, in swagger (openAPI2.0) the schema definition, like bellow.

```yaml
definitions:
  person:
    type: object
    properties:
      name:
        type: string
    additionalPropeties: integer
    required:
      - name
```

Then, about person schema, passing `{"name": "foo"}` is ok, and `{"name": "foo", "x": 20, "y": 10}` is also ok(unexpected field is treated as integer).

it is a bit difficult, making marshmallow's version of this.
(schema meta's additional is candidate of expected field names, ... and so on.)

## proposal

If marshmallow has new hook point that changing the way of iteration, it is enable to implement marshmallow's version, more easily.

For example, `get_marshalling_iterator()`(this is not good name), is existed in Schema class.

```python
# def default_iterator(data, fields_dict):
#     return iteritems(fields_dict)


class S(Schema):
    def get_marshalling_iterator(self, additional_field=fields.Int()):
        def iterate_with_additional_field(data, fields_dict):
            for k in data:
                field = fields_dict.get(k, additional_field)
                yield k, field

        return iterate_with_additional_field

    name = fields.String(required=True)
```

- 💭 get_marshalling_iterator is not good name, maybe
- 💭 the position of hook point(Schema class's instance method) is not good, maybe

issue_title: [windows] Serial Number Issues
issue_body:
For USB composite devices, Pyserial cannot extract extract the serial number. 
What Pyserial needs to do is look at the *parent* device 

When Pyserial extracts the "szHardwareID" - it gets a strings like (see below)
What I am seeing appears to be a "device instance string"
References:

a)  https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/drivers/install/device-instance-ids
b) https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/hardware/jj649944(v=vs.85).aspx

What is needed is this:

Step 1 - if you cannot find the serial number ...

Call the function:  CM_Get_Parent() - pass:  devinfo.DevInst  for the current device.

This gives you the PARENT device ID (this is an ephemeral number - meaning it changes when you reboot the machine, or plug & unplug devices)  

Step 2:

Call the function CM_Get_Device_ID() - pass the PARENT ID  (from above) and a pointer to a buffer to hold the USB string.

Step 3: Inspect the parent USB Vendor ID & USB Product ID - compare with your base child.

Case 1:  
   They do not match - you are done. You have the USB Hub the device is connected to.
   If a serial number exists, it is in the child... 
   Some do not have serial numbers, see COM 114

Case 2:
   They match - but you can't find the serial number - See COM134 as the example.
   Try the parent again... you should only have to go up 2 or 3 times - 4 at most.
   The serial number - if it exists is on the top most element of the matching device.

Case 3:
    You found the serial number - Great success, and Profit!

Below are several examples, note: in this example I am only traversing up 5 levels of parents.

Some notes:

1) it would be helpful if PySerial could identify the MI_NUMBER as an additional attribute
  This corresponds to the USB interface index, not the "NTH-in-some-order"
  It should be the USB interface index number this never changes.
  Rational:  Some devices have multiple serial ports and you need the NTH port...

2) Composite example : COM98 & COM99  are from the same composite device - two interfaces
    (It happens to be a TexasInstruments Launch Pad - they have 6 USB interfaces)

3)  COM96 is a standalone USB cable from FTDI

4) COM147 - is a device from Ember (now SiLabs) it is a non-composite device.
   This is plugged into the side of my laptop which as an (VID_8087 - aka: Intel) USB Hub

5) COM114 - has no serial number, it has a CP110x usb converter
   And it does not have a "MI_XX" number... it is a non-composite device.

6) COM148 - is a an "ARM MBED" device - fairly common development board from ARM
   See:  https://www.mbed.com/en/  - for details

7)  COM150 - is a Linux USB Serial "Gadget" device

8) COM88 is a "Prolific" USB Serial cable

Example parent chains follow

issue_title: Categorical histogram should show percentage in addition to count
issue_body: Currently, the categorical histogram view (`SArray.show` on `str` dtype) shows the absolute value of the count for each category. This may be useful, but the user may be looking instead for the relative value (how much of my dataset has this label?), which percentage would communicate better.

issue_title: warning: 'this' pointer cannot be null in well-defined C++ code; comparison may be assumed to always evaluate to false [-Wtautological-undefined-compare]
issue_body:
We cannot check our headers of warnings and enable -Werror in part because some header files compare `this` against NULL, e.g.
```
/Users/jong/Source/turicreate/src/platform/parallel/pthread_tools.hpp:978:10: warning: 
      'this' pointer cannot be null in well-defined C++ code; comparison may be
      assumed to always evaluate to false [-Wtautological-undefined-compare] 
      if(this == NULL) {
         ^~~~    ~~~~
```
We should remove these redundant checks and enable -Werror=tautological-undefined-compare or -Werror as appropriate

issue_title: DISC Knc out of range
issue_body:
The expression for the DNI clearness index for clear conditions (Knc) is a polynomial fit over the air mass range 1 to 12.  The values of this polynomial increase with air mass values > 17 and reach as high as 6 with air mass 37, which is clearly not realistic.

In the current DISC implementation air mass values up to 37 are allowed, and unrealistic DNI values appear when zenith > 88 / air mass > 19.

I suggest clipping zenith at 85 or 86 instead of clipping cos(zenith) to 0.065.  This produces a more graceful transition to night time.

issue_title: Daylight saving timestamp issue in solarposition.ephemeris
issue_body:
In solarposition.ephemeris:
```
    # make output DataFrame
    DFOut = pd.DataFrame(index=time)
    DFOut['apparent_elevation'] = ApparentSunEl
    DFOut['elevation'] = SunEl
    DFOut['azimuth'] = SunAz
    DFOut['apparent_zenith'] = 90 - ApparentSunEl
    DFOut['zenith'] = 90 - SunEl
    DFOut['solar_time'] = SolarTime
```
I get an error when assigning ``ApparentSunEl`` to the ``DFOut`` column:
```
ValueError: cannot reindex from a duplicate axis
```
and I believe the reason comes from the fact that DFOut uses ``time`` as index and ``ApparentSunEl`` uses ``time_utc`` as index; and I think it raises an error because in my case ``time`` uses daylight saving and contains duplicate indices. Using ``ApparentSunEl.values`` instead should solve the issue.

issue_title: Creation of related resources is not supported
get_serializer_class() should be used to get the serializer_class of views

DRF allows you to override this function and using the attribute directly as in [here](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/d22cc0aa4b338b5c034cb6eefad9162ea81241dd/rest_framework_json_api/utils.py#L59) is a bug.

Now, I want my `Dataset` model to have a `Language` relationship: it should be the list of all (distinct) languages in the sentences of my dataset.

    languages = relations.SerializerMethodResourceRelatedField(

issue_title: Self Referential, ManytoMany, included on create?
issue_body:
We have a model that has a self reference back through itself using `through` and `through_fields`, i.e. 
```python
class Deck(models.Model):
 
    children = models.ManyToManyField(
        'self',
        symmetrical=False,
        related_name='parents',
        through='DeckLineage',
        through_fields=('parent', 'child'),
    )

class DeckLineage(models.Model):
    parent = models.ForeignKey(
        Deck,
        on_delete=models.SET_NULL,
        null=True,
        related_name='when_parent')
    child = models.ForeignKey(
        Deck,
        on_delete=models.SET_NULL,
        null=True,
        related_name='when_child')

```

and we are using the standard serializers, i.e. 
```python
class DeckSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = models.Deck
        fields = '__all__'
```

I'm trying to figure out how to craft a `POST` such that I can specify the `children` relationship in the post so that the `DeckLineage` table gets populated on POST.  Since we have to create these relationships manually in Django (i.e. you can't just say deck.children.add()), I imagine I'll have to write some custom handler. In that case: what is the best practice for handling included relationships on POST like this?

issue_title: `future` not necessarily the best idea
issue_body:
So now that we're on the topic of requirements (#15, #19) -- I'm not sure using `future` in a library like this (esp. `future.standard_library.install_aliases()`) is a good idea.

I mean, just importing `upcloud_api` as it is [will install magical `sys.meta_path` hooks](https://github.com/PythonCharmers/python-future/blob/master/src/future/standard_library/__init__.py#L521-L538).

Maybe just use `six` (as it's a dep too anyway)?

issue_title: DKPY2: Await attributes and parameters
issue_body:
Bikeshedding time~~~!

I feel like we want a method `.await([types], defaults=True)` that does the following:

1. Waits for system_status to enter an initialized state. This is equivalent to a `.notify_change` event on `"initialized"`. (included by default when defaults=True)
1. Waits for parameters to be loaded. This is equivalent to a `.notify_change` event on `"parameters"`. (included by default when defaults=True)
1. Waits for what other defaults? (Armed? Mode? etc.)
1. Waits for an arbitrary list of `.notify_change` events. This encompasses attributes.

So an example vehicle state example may be:

```py
vehicle = connect('127.0.0.1:14550')
vehicle.await(['location', 'attitude', 'velocity', 'gps_0', 'groundspeed',
               'airspeed', 'mount_status', 'battery', 'rangefinder', 'mode', 'armed'])

print " Location: %s" % vehicle.location
print " Attitude: %s" % vehicle.attitude
print " Velocity: %s" % vehicle.velocity
print " GPS: %s" % vehicle.gps_0
print " Groundspeed: %s" % vehicle.groundspeed
print " Airspeed: %s" % vehicle.airspeed
print " Mount status: %s" % vehicle.mount_status
print " Battery: %s" % vehicle.battery
print " Rangefinder: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder
print " Rangefinder distance: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder.distance
print " Rangefinder voltage: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder.voltage
print " Mode: %s" % vehicle.mode.name    # settable
print " Armed: %s" % vehicle.armed    # settable
```

Wow, what a verbose function! But does it make sense? We want to *ensure* these values are populated. But this example then doesn't scale if we are always waiting for a rangefinder event that doesn't happen. Maybe we want to await a selection of always-on parameters, then time-await a selection of properties that may be optional?


```py
vehicle = connect('127.0.0.1:14550')
vehicle.await(['location', 'attitude', 'velocity', 'gps_0', 'groundspeed',
               'airspeed', 'mode', 'armed'])

print " Location: %s" % vehicle.location
print " Attitude: %s" % vehicle.attitude
print " Velocity: %s" % vehicle.velocity
print " GPS: %s" % vehicle.gps_0
print " Groundspeed: %s" % vehicle.groundspeed
print " Airspeed: %s" % vehicle.airspeed
print " Mode: %s" % vehicle.mode.name    # settable
print " Armed: %s" % vehicle.armed    # settable

# optional params
time.sleep(3)

print " Mount status: %s" % vehicle.mount_status
print " Battery: %s" % vehicle.battery
print " Rangefinder: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder
print " Rangefinder distance: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder.distance
print " Rangefinder voltage: %s" % vehicle.rangefinder.voltage
```

We can also consider the `.await()` method to be aliased in the connect function as so:

```py
vehicle = connect('127.0.0.1:14550', await=['location', 'attitude', 'velocity'])
```

(In this case, how do you disable defaults?)

issue_title: send_mavlink() doesn't autofix system id
issue_body:
When sending mavlink messages created with message_factory, the correct system id must be specified, otherwise it won't work.

This was tested using the following code:

```python
 msg = vehicle.message_factory.mount_control_encode(
        0, 0,  # target system, target component
        30 * 100,  # pitch
        0,  # roll
        0,  # yaw
        0)  # save trim
vehicle.send_mavlink(msg)
```

However, changing the system id to the proper value (1 in this case) fixes the problem.
The documentation suggests that this should be automatically taken care of by send_mavlink(), yet it does't seem to be the case.

I tried the same code with dronekit 1 and it does indeed work without the need to specify the sysid manually.

issue_title: Plugins views hide default bucket
issue_body:
Currently, the default bucket views are registered after `cliquet.initialize()`.

The plugin views (`kinto.includes`) are registered during `cliquet.initialize()`.

The plugin views are hence registered before `/buckets/default/*`, and thus hide it.

This is a limitation for *kinto-attachment* which registers `/buckets/{bucket_id}/collections/{collection_id}/records/{id}/attachment`. 

Possible solutions:
* register default bucket views before `cliquet.initialize()`. *Note: version prefix will thus have to be managed explicitly*
* add an *kinto ready* event on which plugins can hook to do last minute config modifications

Thoughts?

issue_title: A2C Loss Function Value vs Policy
issue_body:
The A2C loss function used in this repository is the following:
`        neglogpac = tf.nn.sparse_softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits=train_model.pi, labels=A)

        pg_loss = tf.reduce_mean(ADV * neglogpac)
        entropy = tf.reduce_mean(cat_entropy(train_model.pi))
        vf_loss = tf.reduce_mean(mse(tf.squeeze(train_model.vf), R))
        loss = pg_loss - entropy*ent_coef + vf_loss * vf_coef`
Where there is one loss function for updating all weights in the NN (both value and policy function approximators).

Yet, both [Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction](http://incompleteideas.net/book/bookdraft2017nov5.pdf) and the original A3C paper Mnih [16'] [Asynchronous Methods For Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.01783.pdf) implement two loss functions, one for the value function and one for the policy function:

`w <- w + alpha*discount*advantage*value_gradient`
`theta <- theta + alpha*discount*advantage*log_policy_gradient`

Why does the A2C implementation here only utilize a combined loss function, whereas the literature utilize separate loss functions for the value and policy function approximators?

Use sha as build number in dev pkgs, instead of CI build number
issue_body:
We've been pushing dev pkgs to testpypi using travis for quite a while, but we recently also added standalone binaries that are pushed to an S3 bucket. Those packages need to be created on a like platform (i.e. a Windows pkg needs to be created on a Windows system). So now we have appveyor building Windows standalone pkgs and travis building Linux standalone pkgs.

The build version ends up the same, despite being built in two different places, because we aren't actually setting the build version in the CI jobs for these standalone pkgs.

* https://github.com/plus3it/watchmaker/blob/develop/.travis.yml#L88
* https://github.com/plus3it/watchmaker/blob/develop/appveyor.yml#L61

This leads to a discrepancy between the testpypi dev version and the standalone dev version.

We can align the two by using the sha of the built commit as the build number, for all dev deploys, i.e. `x.x.x.dev<sha>`.

issue_title: spacy pipeline not working for me
issue_body:
I am using WMD-relax with spacy. However, using the following does not trigger the WMD-relax similarity function and still uses the spacy similarity function. 

```
import spacy
import wmd

nlp = spacy.load('en', create_pipeline=wmd.WMD.create_spacy_pipeline)
doc1 = nlp("Politician speaks to the media in Illinois.")
doc2 = nlp("The president greets the press in Chicago.")
print(doc1.similarity(doc2))

```
It produces exactly the same similarity measures which spacy normal similarity function does:

```
 def similarity(self, other):
        """
        Compute a semantic similarity estimate. Defaults to cosine over vectors.
        Arguments:
            other:
                The object to compare with. By default, accepts Doc, Span,
                Token and Lexeme objects.
        Returns:
            score (float): A scalar similarity score. Higher is more similar.
        """
        if 'similarity' in self.doc.user_token_hooks:
                return self.doc.user_token_hooks['similarity'](self)
        if self.vector_norm == 0 or other.vector_norm == 0:
            return 0.0
        return numpy.dot(self.vector, other.vector) / (self.vector_norm * other.vector_norm)
```

For now, I am using the following as a workaround which works fine. 
wmd_instance = SpacySimilarityHook(nlp)
wmd_instance.compute_similarity(doc1, doc2).  # it returns the distance

Any ideas why the pipeline isn't triggering the wmd-relax similarity function?

Another thing which I observed is that it doesn't run on certain CPU unless built from source. It's a minor thing.

Therefore we've implemented Django Axes a bit differently than normal.

https://github.com/jazzband/django-axes/issues/246

Regards,

Roland

tldr: Use semantic versioning.

issue_title: Run custom commands before to build
issue_body:
Hi!

When I run my jobs using Docker, sometimes I need to install some specific package by APT (e.g. subversion), or execute some non-trivial script. The problem is that all docker commands executed to build/test/upload my packages are provided by package tools. So, it's not possible to add any additional entrypoint before build.

For now, I need to create custom docker images to add new layers including my dependencies.

My suggestion is support some custom script to be executed as *entrypoint*:

```python
import os
from conan.packager import ConanMultiPackager

if __name__ == "__main__":
    builder = ConanMultiPackager(entrypoint=os.path.join("entrypoint", "install.py") # could be .sh
    builder.add_common_builds()
    builde.run()
```

When `entrypoint` is present, the script on path is executed once by `self.run()`, before to create temporary profile or create the packages.

Regards!

issue_title: Cfn-Sphere fails to update Stacks with nested stacks
issue_body: If cfn-sphere has to wait on create_complete or update_complete events for stacks containing nested stacks, it finishes to early. Reason is a bug in the event filtering. We currently only wait for events of type "AWS::CloudFormation::Stack" with status "*_COMPLETE". When a nested stack resource completes it's creation, an event matching the given criteria occurs for the root stack. The only difference is the logical resource id stating that this event is for the nested stack.

issue_title: For XLSX files, iget_records seems to load the whole file in memory before it starts to return content
issue_body:
I just tried this with a 30 MB Excel file of around 500k rows:

    with open('big_excel_file.xlsx') as f:

issue_title: How to use sparse tensor as input data properly?
issue_body:
Hi, all

I'm trying to input sparse data as X in order to use `embedding_lookup_sparse`, but seems not that easy to add this feature to tflearn.

The largest problem I found now is the feeding logic: When I'm trying to input sparse tensor for X and dense tensor for y, the feeding logic could not  handle batch properly in this case. It requires the X and y has the same length (aka. shape[0]), but since the X is a sparse tensor, I have to make batches of myself when generating SparseTensorValue. 

So I want to know what is the right way to do that in current version of tflearn? Or it's just a "not supported yet" requirement?

Thanks.

issue_title: Crashing galore with convolutional autoencoder

I'm trying to create a convolutional autoencoder (which, when stable, will hopefully be pegged for submission to /examples/).

However, I'm always getting NaN between my highest conv_2d layer and lowest conv_2d_transpose layer. Sometimes it's gradients, though the flavor of the last dozen crashes or so is the good ole ReLU. I've tried quite a few things (learning rate [currently .001], different bias/weight init patterns, a variety of activation functions, differing layer depths, batch sizes 1-8, etc.). However, it doesn't seem to even make it to 100 iterations. The data's just imagenet in the people class, and my loss is reasonable (between .02 and .006--the loss for the particular problem I'm working on is lower than standard.)

Maybe it's poor network design, but I'm not fully convinced yet that that's the case. Regardless of my configuration, the lowest conv_2d_transpose layer always is the one that triggers the NaN. Once or twice it was the second lowest, but that was 2. when I cranked up the learning rates very high to watch the gradients for distinct patterns and 2. when my network was ~30 layers deep. I've had to wrangle the conv_2d_transpose layer a bit to get it to cooperate fully, and plan on trying to expand that functionality within tflearn once I get some free cycles. network = input_data(shape=[None, 224, 224, 3])
network = conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 32, [3, 3, 32, 64], [1, 1, 1, 1], [112, 112, 32], activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 16, [3, 3, 16, 32], [1, 2, 2, 1], [224, 224, 16], activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 2, [3, 3, 2, 16], [1, 1, 1, 1], [224, 224, 2], activation='sigmoid', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')

issue_title: Sphinx autodoc API documentation is currently broken
issue_body:
All of the embedded *autodoc* API documentation is missing from the [cmd2 docs](http://cmd2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).

Typically when this occurs it means that we have added a new required dependency and we forgot to add that to the documentation [requirements.txt](https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/blob/master/docs/requirements.txt)

This same issue has occurred a number of times before.  We should consider trying to add a unit test for the build of the Sphinx HTML documentation to make sure some of the API documentation is present and/or that the docs can build with no significant errors/warnings.

issue_title: Consider adding a feature to support submenus
issue_body:
In some types of interactive command-line applications it is desirable to have stateful behavior where the commands available depend on the state and you effectively have sub-menus.

This should not be the "default" behavior in ``cmd2``.  But it would be nice to have it as an optional opt-in behavior through something like a decorator-based approach.

issue_title: Set AnymailTrackingEvent.metadata to empty dict instead of None
issue_body:
If there is no metadata attached a tracking event the metadata field is set to `None`. This is a bit inconvenient while handling tracking events as it introduces an extra conditional before accessing the datastructure:

```
if event.metadata:
  value = event.metadata.get(key)
```

Setting `metadata = {}` instead removes the need for the conditional.

issue_title: binding to api that that overloads allocation/deallocation operators
issue_body:
I'm trying to create a python binding for libdar-2.5.4 - a c++11 library that creates archives in a similar manner to tar. Most of the classes I want to bind inherit from a class called on_pool - it uses the new [] operator to overload the construction 
http://dar.sourceforge.net/doc/html/classlibdar_1_1on__pool.html
you can see the code here:
http://dar.sourceforge.net/doc/html/on__pool_8hpp_source.html
]
I'm having trouble knowing how to bind to classes that inherit from on_pool - or whether in fact its a smart thing to do. For example, if I bind bool_mask:
py::class_<libdar::bool_mask>(m, "bool_mask").def(py::init<bool>());
 .. I'll get this error
pybind11.h:978:67: error: no matching function for call to 'libdar::bool_mask::operator new(sizetype, libdar::bool_mask*&)
Currently I'm thinking of writing sibling classes for the on_pool-inherited classes I want to bind and copy from one to the other - its not as efficient but will get around the problem. It would be nice to know whether its possible/sane to attempt to bind directly when the constructor overloading stuff is going on.
